---

## 3. How work begins

A new product effort starts one of three ways:

1. **A customer request** — a retail buyer asks sales for a specific product type, format, or a refresh of an existing line. Sales relays the brief to the PM.
2. **An internal line refresh** — the company decides on its own to refresh a line (new designs, new licensors or properties, a new product category) and presents it to buyers afterward.
3. **New product development** — the team develops a brand-new product format or category into a full concept before seeking buyer interest.

Where the *ideas* come from (Jessica): "a shopping trip to one of the retailers our client or we want it to be, an offer from a factory, inspiration from internet, a mix from existent product formats we already have."

**Two secondary sales channels exist alongside direct retail:**
- **Wholesale sublicensors** — online sellers (e.g., Stallion Art, Iconick) buy the product and resell it under their own brand, sublicensing the IP. Their designs still go through the **full licensor approval process**. These are designed by outside teams, which makes them unusually hard to manage (see §9).
- **Internal / new development** — products built proactively, then presented.

The old ClickUp data showed the same flows repeating across thousands of records. The important lesson was not each individual row; it was the **shape** of the work. Lists that looked like flat task boards were actually mixtures of parent project cards and child SKU/product tasks. For example, the largest licensed list contained 7,281 parent cards and 4,280 child tasks, while a buyer-refresh list contained only 264 parent cards but 2,446 child tasks. That proved that the business does not run on one generic "task" object. It runs on linked projects, products, and reusable creative work.

The monitoring also showed that a lot of work is not visible as "moving a stage." Much of the real activity is editing descriptions, attaching images, pasting file paths, adding comments, changing assignments, and updating content. A person new to the business should understand that **status columns tell only part of the story**; the files, notes, specs, and handoffs are often where the work really happens.

---

# PART A — POP CREATIONS: The Licensed Product Journey

## 4. The three things the business is really tracking

Everything in POP is one of three objects.

**An offer (a "project").** One offer, to one buyer, at one retailer, for one season. For example: *"Julie Greer at Burlington for Valentines 2027,"* or *"Alice Zhu at Dollar General for Fall/Winter 2026."* An offer carries the **brief**: the buyer, the retailer, the season, which licensors and properties are in play, which product types the buyer wants, the on-shelf date, and any restrictions. It exists so the creative team has a single reference and doesn't have to memorize the details. As Jessica put it, "each buyer purchases certain types of products and specific properties within each license. All this information is included… as a brief so the creative designer can refer to it during the process."

The brief carries real constraints: **the style guides used to source the design assets must be valid for the date the product will be on the shelf** — licensors date-gate their style guides to selling seasons.

**A picked product (a "SKU").** When a buyer selects a design from a presentation, that selection usually becomes a specific product to be built, carried all the way through approval and production. More precisely, a SKU is created whenever something must be submitted to a licensor's system; that can be a buyer pick, an internal concept, a sample-first effort, or any other item the company wants the licensor to approve. A picked product carries: its full approval history, its link back to the offer it came from, its licensor submission numbers, its sample photos, and the order history of which retailers bought it and when.

There is also a third, quieter category the business currently *loses*: **the preliminary designs that were shown to a buyer but not picked.** These are real creative work, but today they vanish (see §10 — this is the company's single biggest pain point).

## 5. The full journey, step by step

Here is the path a licensed product travels, and who touches it at each step.

| # | Step | Who does it | What happens |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brief / offer created** | Sales + PM | An offer is defined for a buyer/retailer/season with its restrictions, product types, properties, and dates. |
| 2 | **Preliminary designs created** | Creative Designer | The creative team designs concept options for the buyer, working from the brief. |
| 3 | **Presentation to buyer** | Sales (Adam) | Sales shows the preliminary designs to the buyer. |
| 4 | **Buyer picks + format chosen** | Buyer | The buyer selects designs and picks the **format** for each — material, size, specifications. Only now does a pick become a product to build. |
| 5 | **Product (SKU) created + description written** | Creative Designer | Each pick, or any other concept that needs licensor approval, becomes a tracked product. The creative designer writes the description (material, size, artwork description) and prepares the final art files. |
| 6 | **Licensing sheet + packaging built** | Technical Designer | Using the art files, the technical designer builds the **licensing sheet** (the formal spec the licensor reviews) and designs the packaging. |
| 7 | **Internal Creative Director review** | Creative Director (Liz) | **Nothing goes to a licensor without Liz's approval.** She reviews the licensing sheet and the design. This is the internal gate. |
| 8 | **Concept submitted to licensor** | Licensing Team | The licensing team submits the concept and packaging to the licensor's portal. |
| 9 | **Licensor responds** | Licensor | The licensor either **approves**, **rejects with revisions**, or **approves with changes** (approved, but minor revisions are required before sampling). |
| 10a | **Order placed (PO received)** | Buyer → Technical Designer | The buyer places a purchase order; files are prepared for the factory. |
| 10b | **OR sample requested by sales** | Sales → Technical Designer | No order yet, but sales wants a physical sample to close the deal; files are prepared for the factory. *(10a and 10b are parallel paths into sampling.)* |
| 11 | **Sample requested from factory** | Factory | Tech packs are sent to a factory, which produces a pre-production sample (PPS). |
| 12 | **Sample reviewed internally** | Creative Director + Licensing Team | The factory sends PPS photos. Liz and the licensing team review them before anything goes back to the licensor. |
| 13 | **Factory resample (if needed)** | Factory | If errors are found, the sample is corrected and re-photographed. |
| 14 | **Sample submitted to licensor (PPS)** | Licensing Team | A clean sample is submitted to the licensor for PPS approval. |
| 15 | **Sample revision (if licensor requests)** | Creative + Technical Designer | The licensor may request changes to the sample. |
| 16 | **Pre-production approved (PPS approved)** | Licensor | The licensor approves the sample. **Mass production is now authorized.** |
| 17 | **Mass production** | Factory | The factory produces the order. |
| 18 | **Production approved / closed** | Licensing Team | All licensor requirements are satisfied and the submission is closed. *Requirements vary by licensor* — some need a product-safety report, some need the physical sample shipped to their office, some are satisfied with PPS approval alone. |
| 19 | **Shipping & import** | Production Team | The licensor's submission form (the "Brand Assurance" record) is printed and attached to the shipping boxes alongside the trademark authorization, so the goods can ship and clear import. |

A product is **"approved"** at step 9 only when the licensor returns it with no feedback or corrections. A product is **"finished"** at step 18 (Production Approved).

The old status names preserve an important business truth: not every open item is truly active work. Some items are actively moving, some are waiting on a buyer, some are waiting on a licensor, some are waiting on factory information, some are approved-but-unsold inventory, and some are effectively abandoned but never formally closed. The business needs those lifecycle meanings because "open" alone is too vague.

## 6. The licensor relationship in depth

This is the defining feature of the licensed line. Two separate approvals are required: **concept approval** (before any sample is made) and **sample / PPS approval** (before mass production).

**What a licensor checks at concept (Jessica):**
- Correct use of assets — no mixing different style guides, no distorting or modifying the characters.
- Use of color.
- **Storytelling** — the design must tell a story; no plain backgrounds, no unrelated assets thrown together.
- Packaging against the licensor's guidelines.
- The on-shelf date — the style guide used must be valid for the selling window.

**Who talks to the licensor:** only the licensing team (manager + coordinator). Packaging revisions are handled by technical design; creative revisions by the creative designer. **Everything the licensing team submits is first approved internally by Liz.**

**Licensors respond at very different speeds**, which materially affects planning. Expected turnaround per licensor (Jessica):

| Licensor | Expected response |
|---|---|
| Disney | 1–3 days |
| LucasFilm / Star Wars | 1–2 days |
| Marvel | 1–2 days |
| Nickelodeon (Paramount) | 3–6 days |
| Sesame Street | 4 days |
| Coca-Cola | 6 days |
| NBC Universal | 5–7 days |
| Warner Brothers | 5–10 days |
| Peanuts | 7–10 days |
| SEGA | 7–10 days |
| Strawberry Shortcake (WildBrain CPLP) | 7–10 days |
| WWE | 7–10 days |
| Care Bears | 7–10 days |
| One Piece (TOEI) | 7–15 days |

Some licensors are also far more demanding on revisions than others — SEGA in particular tends to require at least one concept revision on essentially everything, while Disney and Star Wars approve most concepts cleanly.

### Compliance artifacts

**Brand Assurance.** When a new item is submitted, the licensor's portal generates a **submission number** — the Brand Assurance number. The licensing team records it (linking it to the product) and saves the submission PDF. It's needed a *second* time at production: the production team prints the Brand Assurance form and pastes it on the shipping boxes alongside the trademark authorization so the goods can be imported. So it is **both a submission record and a shipping-compliance document.** *(Notably, Liz — the Creative Director — has never heard of it; it lives entirely with the licensing team.)*

**PI / Product Integrity.** A test report certifying that the materials used (and their quantities) are non-toxic, non-hazardous, and safe. **Only some licensors require it.** When required, the licensing team receives the report by email and submits it to the licensor. Because most licensors don't require it, it is tracked inconsistently today.

Wholesale sublicensor products create a special strain on this approval process. They still need licensor approval, but the creative work comes from outside teams that often do not understand licensing guidelines. Liz still has to review and give feedback, but she is correcting people she does not manage, which makes the work slower and more difficult than normal retail product development.

## 7. Costing, sourcing, and manufacturing constraints

Before a product can be quoted or built, a **costing sheet** is created — and this is the source of a lot of downstream rework.

- A **technical designer** creates the costing sheet using internet references, photos from shopping trips, and product offers from factories.
- The **art director (Liz) approves the costing sheet** before it goes to factories for quotes.
- The **sourcing team / China team** reviews it to confirm or clarify restrictions, construction, and materials, and **obtains the die lines**.

The problem: the **manufacturing constraints** — die lines, the number of colors a factory can print, the printing technique, where legal lines must be placed — are often **known only to the art director**, who "usually specifies the restrictions, but sometimes forgets to share them with the creative team, or sometimes she's unaware of them." The mismatch is then discovered during sampling, forcing the design and licensing sheet to be redone. When both the creative and technical designers understand the product-type constraints up front (based on the agreed factory cost), products go through with far fewer revisions.

This is not just a documentation problem. It is a visibility problem: designers need manufacturing constraints early enough to design correctly, while pricing and sensitive costing information still belongs with sourcing, sales, and leadership. The business needs to separate **constraints designers need** from **prices designers should not see**.

## 8. How long the work takes

The team has internal time estimates for each design stage, and they **depend on the product type**. These are the company's own expectations for how long good work should take (in minutes):

| Product Type | Brief | Design | Art File | Licensing Sheet | Revisions | Tech Pack for Factory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stretched/Box | 10 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Framed | 10 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Plaque | 10 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Functional | 20 | 30 | 60 | 150 | 60 | 48 |
| Other Wall | 20 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Block | 10 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Box | 10 | 30 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Photo Frames | 30 | 60 | 60 | 150 | 60 | 48 |
| Object | 30 | 60 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Other Tabletop | 20 | 60 | 30 | 75 | 30 | 24 |
| Clocks | 10 | 30 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Soft Storage | 30 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Hard Storage | 30 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Other Storage | 20 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Stationery Org | 30 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Desk Acc | 20 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Other Workspace | 20 | 60 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |
| Floor Coverings | 10 | 15 | 20 | 50 | 20 | 16 |
| Garden | 30 | 30 | 40 | 100 | 40 | 32 |

Combined with the per-licensor response times in §6, these define what "on track" means for any product working backward from its on-shelf date.

## 9. Reuse, multi-buyer dynamics, and closure

**Selling the same product to more than one buyer.** Most licensors allow a product to be sold to multiple buyers over time. **Some buyers prohibit resale** of what they've bought — a restriction the business must respect per buyer. There is also a **time constraint** on re-offering. Today reuse happens only case-by-case and only occasionally, when someone remembers the product exists. The product keeps its identity (its code is the licensor's approval record) and should carry the history of which retailers ordered it.

**Two buyers pick the same design at once.** When this happens, the team notices manually. The fix: the creative designer makes a **second version with minor changes** (different icons, image sizes, colors, or embellishments), and sales presents the variation to one of the two buyers — without explaining the real reason.

**Closing or canceling a product.** A product should be canceled, with a reason, when:
- the factory quote doesn't meet the company's cost target (**cost**),
- the product isn't under contract with the licensor (**licensing**),
- a manufacturing problem was found during sampling — construction impossible, too difficult, or too expensive (**sampling**),
- the buyer declined and no other buyer is interested (**buyer**).

A product that reaches Production Approved is **completed** — the happy path. The business problem today: there is **no formal way to cancel**, so dead products sit open indefinitely, some for four to five years.

**Approved-but-unsold concepts.** A large pool of concepts get licensor approval but then stall — the buyer passed on price, the production timing didn't fit, or the order was canceled. These are **not dead**; they are approved inventory that should be re-offered to buyers, but today the work is effectively wasted because nobody re-surfaces it.

This is one of the most important distinctions in the business: **canceled**, **abandoned**, **complete**, **parked**, and **reusable** are not the same state. A canceled product may be dead because cost, licensing, sampling, or buyer interest failed. An approved-but-unsold concept is different: the company already paid for creative and licensor approval, so it may be valuable inventory for a future buyer.

## 10. Where the POP process breaks today (process problems, in the team's words)

**1. Lost preliminary designs — the #1 pain point.** Designs shown to a buyer but not picked are tied to that one buyer's presentation and effectively disappear. *"Designs that are lost are lost several times a week. We've tried to reuse these designs for other projects… but it involves manually searching through past presentations"* (Jessica). The team only ever organizes what was *selected*; the rest is lost creative work.

**2. The Creative Director bottleneck.** Liz's review-and-approval step is the structural chokepoint, for five distinct reasons (Jessica):
1. **Volume** — licensing sheets pile up when Liz is busy with her other responsibilities.
2. **Late design changes** — Liz sometimes changes artwork she already approved at the presentation stage, which forces the entire licensing sheet to be rebuilt.
3. **Product-type unfamiliarity** — she sometimes has to consult senior creative, sales, production, or sourcing in China before she can approve a product type, which adds days.
4. **Color preferences** — colors the creative designer explicitly called out get sent back for her preference.
5. **Property / packaging mismatch** — the wrong property gets used (e.g., "Mickey and Friends" art on a "Mickey Mouse" submission), and the work is returned until corrected.

**3. Capacity and surges.** Jessica's core daily job is allocating limited designers across competing deadlines. Surges break this: a flood of Hobby Lobby picks once outran the creative team's ability to deliver art (worsened by unfamiliar new product types), forcing overtime, pulling creatives off other projects, and sending samples *without* approvals to keep up; a huge Ollies order forced pausing everything else.

**4. Knowledge trapped in people's heads.** *"Information is very much in people's heads. Communication isn't this company's strong suit, and records of certain things are scattered everywhere… Many people don't understand the complete workflow… sometimes there are rework processes because the wrong person does the task"* (Jessica).

**5. Unclear ownership of scheduling.** The art director sometimes assigns tasks and projects, which is the PM's role, *"which throws off my deadlines"* (Jessica). (This is a genuine tension, not just a mistake — Liz legitimately knows which designer is strong at which product, so she does help direct work.)

**6. Manufacturing constraints surface too late.** As in §7 — constraints known only to the art director aren't shared with designers until sampling reveals the mismatch.

**7. The work record is scattered across several places.** ClickUp may show the stage, but the reasons and details often live in Teams messages, emails, Illustrator markups, NAS paths, PDFs, screenshots, costing tools, or someone's memory. That scattering is why the business repeatedly loses context even when a product card technically exists.

**8. Activity is not the same as stage movement.** The monitoring showed frequent description/content edits, attachments, comments, and assignment changes. Those are not administrative noise; they are how product work actually advances. A business process that only watches stage changes will miss a lot of the real work.

## 11. How Jessica and Liz wish the POP process ran

- **Nothing creative is ever lost.** Every design — picked or not — stays findable and reusable, searchable by license, property, product type, and season, so the team can re-offer past work instead of starting from scratch.
- **The business gets ahead of the season instead of scrambling.** *"Project management should be proactive, not reactive… Instead of waiting for a buyer to request products for next year's Valentine's Day or Christmas and then scrambling, we should have offered the products beforehand"* (Jessica). The company already knows its seasons — Valentine's, Easter, Graduation, Back to School, Harvest, Halloween, Christmas, plus Everyday — and its history of which buyers order what. Approved-but-unsold concepts should be proactively packaged into seasonal offers.
- **Designers see their constraints while they design.** Die lines, color counts, printing techniques, and materials should be visible at design time, so the art director is no longer a single point of relay and rework drops.
- **Each person owns and advances their own step**, with clear handoffs to the next person — so the PM manages exceptions instead of pushing every product through every stage by hand. For this to work, updating has to be effortless and incremental (you report the first 5 of 20 art files as they're done, not all 20 at the end).
- **Liz keeps her judgment but loses the chaos.** She doesn't want a rigid checklist imposed on her aesthetic review — that runs on 20 years of expertise. She *does* want her submissions tracked in one place instead of scattered across messages, and she wants product specs (how each product is made) in front of her when she reviews.
- **Lifecycle states are explicit.** A product should be visibly active, waiting, parked, reusable, canceled, abandoned, or complete, instead of quietly sitting in an old status for years.

---

# PART B — SPRUCE LINE: The Generic Product Journey

*Spruce Line (formerly "Edge") is the company's non-licensed, original-design business. There is no licensor anywhere in this process — the only external approval is the buyer's.*

## 12. The three things the business is tracking

Spruce is organized differently from POP. Jen tracks three distinct kinds of thing:

**A design collection (a trend/art theme).** A body of original art on a theme — "Gaming," "Farmhouse," "Cowgirl Country," "Soft Religion" — that is **available to all accounts**, not tied to any one buyer. Collections are organized by format and then by theme (e.g., under "Wall Art": Gaming, Farmhouse, Kitchen Art, Sneaker Art, Automotive, Florals, Coastal, Abstracts, and many more; under "Floor Coverings": Coir Mats, Spring Coir Mats, Holiday Coir Mats). A single trend collection can hold **hundreds of designs**, and the **high-resolution art isn't even produced until a buyer asks for it.** Collections are the raw creative inventory; they are not yet products.

**A project (account-specific work).** One project for one buyer/account — for example, "Forman Mills, buyer Jennifer, Wall Art" or "Burlington, buyer Anna, New Formats." A project is where a specific buyer's selections and brief live.

**A style-numbered product.** When a buyer actually commits — selects specific designs and either places an order or requests a sample — those selections become **style-numbered products**. The style number is the product's permanent identity, and all of that product's files are findable by it. **Only style-numbered items are "real products."** The hundreds of designs in a trend collection are not.

This is the biggest Spruce nuance: a trend collection may contain hundreds of useful designs, but that does not mean hundreds of products exist. A product exists only once an account-specific commitment creates a style number or sample/order path. That is why a searchable library for Spruce cannot assume every design already has production-ready art; many concepts are still only presentation-level creative inventory.

## 13. The journey, step by step

| # | Step | Who | What happens |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theme/collection development** | Jen + design team | The team develops trend and art themes. They meet twice a month — once for art, once for product — to decide what to develop. Jen sends a recap of who works on what. |
| 2 | **AI art screening** | Nat (Junior Designer) | For AI-generated art, Nat removes poor-quality pieces and obvious AI mistakes before Jen reviews. |
| 3 | **Presentation finalized** | Jen | Jen finalizes the presentation before it goes to sales. |
| 4 | **Presented to buyer** | Adam (Sales) | Adam presents to the buyer and gathers any requested art changes. |
| 5 | **Selections captured** | Jen + team | After the meeting the team builds a **selections document** of everything the buyer chose. |
| 6 | **Account-specific next step** | varies by account | What happens next depends entirely on the account (see §14). |
| 7 | **Style number assigned (commitment)** | Jen + team | Once the buyer commits to a size/format and an order (or a sample for sample-accounts), each selection gets a **style number** — it becomes a real product. |
| 8 | **Costing** | Design → Albert/China → Adam | Design makes a costing tech pack; Albert and the China team handle factory selection and costing; **Adam reviews and approves the costing**. |
| 9 | **Sample (if the account requires one)** | Mal | Mal approves sample requests. Some accounts get samples, some don't. |
| 10 | **Order → production** | Factory (via Yuchen) | When there's an order, the team waits for **Yuchen's email** with the factory deadline, then preps and sends files for production. |

**Approvals are entirely internal + buyer.** Jen finalizes; Adam gets buyer sign-off on art changes; once the buyer approves and an order is placed, it goes to production. **Jen and Mal review artwork and tech packs**; Jen handles purchase-order review for accuracy; Mal handles sample-request approvals. There is no licensor anywhere.

In Spruce, the board is organized by lifecycle stage because Jen needs to see what needs follow-up next, not because every account follows an identical path. Some labels are true process stages, while others are category-like buckets such as Wall Art, Floor Coverings, Storage, or Seasonal. That mixture is part of how the line actually operates: category, account, and lifecycle are all relevant at once.

## 14. Accounts behave differently — and the process must flex

The unit of work after a buyer meeting depends on the account. Two real examples (Jen):

- **Burlington (buyer Anna) — no samples.** Meeting → selections document → Anna uses it to make her final selections for an order → **only then** are style numbers assigned. This account never gets samples.
- **Hobby Lobby (buyer Kyle) — samples required.** Meeting → selections document → the team internally picks a size and format → Kyle approves or requests changes → style numbers are assigned → **samples are requested.**

Most trend collections are **not** account-specific — **except all storage designs and all Hobby Lobby work**, which are. This account-by-account variation is exactly why Jen organizes her work by **lifecycle stage** rather than by buyer: at a glance she needs to see where each piece of work is and what the next step is.

## 15. Seasons, cancellation, reuse, and cross-line flow

**Seasons are account-specific.** Spruce does *not* follow POP's fixed retail calendar. Hobby Lobby works to a dated calendar; Burlington requests verbally at meetings. The team updates its seasonal presentations **starting with Hobby Lobby** and adapts from there.

**Cancellation.** The most common reason a Spruce design never reaches production is simply that **the buyer doesn't move forward** — projects sit "with the buyer for approval" for weeks or months. Adam and Jen review the full list **once a quarter** and remove the stalled ones. As with POP, there's no formal cancel step.

**Reuse.** Design collections are reused across all accounts by design — that's their entire purpose. Once something is a style-numbered product it belongs to a specific buyer, and multi-buyer reuse isn't tracked the way it is in POP.

**Reactive vs. proactive.** Usually the buyer reviews themes the company has already developed and selects from them; occasionally a buyer asks for a specific theme the company doesn't have yet (e.g., "Bears with Sweaters").

**Cross-line.** The two businesses **share product development but not art** — a product format might be shared, but licensed and generic artwork stay separate.

**General presentations.** Spruce maintains account-agnostic presentation boards so Adam can self-serve material for sales conversations. These are organized by areas such as New Formats, Trend Boards, Garden, Floor Coverings, Storage, Seasonal, Wall Decor, Wall Art, and General. They are not the same as account projects, even when they later feed one.

**Failed experiments.** Not every historical board represents a live process. The "Freelancers Generic" board was an attempt to manage freelance art development, but the work became too inconsistent and hard to track. Most of that model was abandoned or pulled back in-house. A new person should not treat every old board as a process to preserve.

## 16. Where the Spruce process breaks today

- **Chasing people.** Jen's single biggest frustration: *"Having to chase down information or remind people to complete their part of the product lifecycle."*
- **Factory opacity.** Factories aren't transparent about their capabilities, and sometimes **change pricing during the pricing stage in ways that aren't revealed until samples have already been requested** — wasting work.
- **No timeline tracking.** The design team doesn't track order timelines at all; the only hard date is the factory deadline that arrives in Yuchen's email. Buyers (especially Burlington) often place orders at the last minute, leaving little prep time.
- **No revision tracking.** Buyer change requests travel by message, marked-up file, or email, with nothing formally recorded.
- **A failed freelancer experiment.** An attempt to develop new art through many freelancers became unmanageable; most work was brought back in-house and the freelancer pool was culled to a few reliable people.
- **Presentation-level work can be mistaken for product work.** Because general collections, account projects, selections PDFs, and style-numbered products all coexist, it is easy to overcount what is actually in production. Spruce needs the distinction between "creative inventory," "buyer project," and "real product" to stay clear.

## 17. How Jen wishes the Spruce process ran

- **Everyone on one consistent system, and sticking to it.** *"Having all team members using the same system for tracking and sticking with it would make everything more organized."* The discipline matters as much as the tool.
- **Sales can self-serve project status.** A way to give Adam *"the exact status of all projects… so he can easily get answers to his questions"* without Jen assembling it by hand.
- **Get ahead of demand with a design calendar built from history.** Use the timing of past years' projects to anticipate when each account will need what, instead of reacting.
- **Honest, shared visibility into timing** — how long pricing and samples actually take — so the burden of chasing doesn't fall on the design team alone.

Her model of a frictionless account is **Ollies**: the buyer buys everything he selects, works in a single format (canvas), pricing already exists, and he communicates well. The more the process can make every account behave like that, the better.

---

# PART C — Where the Two Lines Meet

## 18. Shared structure, shared ailments

Although POP and Spruce run different processes, they share people (Adam in sales; Albert and the China team for costing and factories; Yuchen for factory deadlines), share product development (but never artwork), and suffer several of the **same underlying process ailments**:

| Ailment | POP (Jessica/Liz) | Spruce (Jen) |
|---|---|---|
| **Creative work / status gets lost or scattered** | preliminary designs lost; records spread across messages, email, and shared servers | chasing people for status; nothing in one place |
| **Stalled work piles up with no formal closure** | approved-but-unsold concepts; products open 4–5 years | projects sit "with buyer" for months; quarterly manual cull |
| **No record of revisions** | feedback lives in messages and markups | change requests live in messages and emails |
| **Reacting instead of planning the season** | scrambling each season | wants a design calendar from history |
| **Constraints/pricing surface too late** | manufacturing constraints known only to the art director | factory pricing changes hidden until sampling |
| **Sales needs a clean status picture** | "list of all projects for the same retailer" | "a list for Adam on the exact status of all projects" |
| **The old tracking mixed business concepts** | project cards, SKU cards, admin tasks, tags, and stale statuses lived together | collections, account projects, category buckets, selections, and style-numbered products lived together |
| **File and note context matters as much as stage** | art files, licensing sheets, NAS paths, PDFs, and markups carry the actual work | presentations, selections PDFs, Teams/email revisions, and factory deadline emails carry the actual work |

The deepest shared wish across all three people is the same: **one place where the work lives, nothing gets lost, and the team can get ahead of the season instead of chasing it.**

The deepest shared modeling lesson is also the same: the business needs to know **what kind of thing** it is looking at before it can manage it. A project, a product, a design, a collection, a submission, a sample, and a file handoff are related, but they are not interchangeable.

---

## Appendix — Glossary

- **Licensor** — the IP owner (Disney, Marvel, etc.) that must approve licensed designs and samples. POP only.
- **Property** — a specific franchise/character set within a licensor (e.g., "Mickey Mouse" vs. "Mickey and Friends").
- **Style guide** — the licensor's rulebook for using its assets, gated to selling seasons by date.
- **Project / offer** — one offer to one buyer at one retailer for one season (POP); one account-specific body of work (Spruce).
- **SKU** (POP) — a specific picked product carried through approval and production.
- **Style number** (Spruce) — the permanent identity of a committed Spruce product.
- **Design collection** (Spruce) — an account-agnostic trend/art theme containing many designs.
- **Licensing sheet** — the formal product spec the licensor reviews. POP only.
- **Brand Assurance** — the licensor's submission number; also the compliance form attached to shipping boxes. POP only.
- **PI / Product Integrity** — a materials-safety test report some licensors require. POP only.
- **PPS** — pre-production sample; the physical sample the licensor approves before mass production. POP only.
- **On-shelf date** — when a product must be in stores. **PPS-requested date** — when a buyer wants to see the physical sample (often weeks earlier). Two different deadlines.
- **Costing sheet** — the document that establishes what a product will cost to make, built before factory quotes.

input.description: Deep-dive DesignFlow licensing tracking
input.prompt:
You are analyzing the "licensing tracking" feature of the DesignFlow PLM application (a multi-repo Angular + Node/Express + PostgreSQL system). Produce a DETAILED, EVIDENCE-BACKED technical report. Cite file paths (and line numbers where useful). Do NOT modify any files — read only.

Repos (all on D:\repos\dflow\):
- designflow-frontend (Angular 21, AG Grid Enterprise) — port 4200
- designflow-tracking (Node/Express + PostgreSQL + Sequelize) — port 5002
- designflow-backend (Node/Express + PostgreSQL + Sequelize) — port 5000
- designflow-item-master (5003), designflow-bff (5004 proxy), designflow-data-syncing (5001)

PRIMARY TARGET: the licensing tracking module at
  designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/
especially licensing_tracking.component.ts, .html, the module, and its AG Grid column config (search designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/ for a licensing-tracking grid config).

Investigate and report on:

1. PURPOSE & UI: What does the licensing tracking screen actually do? What is the grid showing — one row per what business object (a licensor submission? a product/SKU? a style number)? What columns/fields exist (list the meaningful ones, grouped: identity, licensor/property, dates, status/stage, sample/PPS, compliance like Brand Assurance / PI, pricing, factory/vendor, files/images)? What actions can a user take (status changes, image upload, vendor assignment, dialogs, exports)?

2. DATA MODEL: Which backend serves this screen? Trace the API service methods it calls (look in designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts and any other service) and map them to backend endpoints/controllers/routes and Sequelize models/DB tables (search designflow-tracking and designflow-backend models/). List the actual table/model names and their key columns relevant to licensing. Note the licensor submission / Brand Assurance / sample / approval fields if present.

3. WORKFLOW MAPPING: Map what this screen tracks to the real licensor approval business process: brief → preliminary design → buyer pick → SKU → licensing sheet → Liz internal review → concept submitted to licensor → licensor responds (approve/revise) → PO or sample request → factory sample (PPS) → internal review → PPS submitted to licensor → PPS approved → mass production → production approved/closed → shipping (Brand Assurance form). Which of these steps does the DesignFlow licensing tracking screen actually capture, and which does it NOT capture?

4. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER TRACKING SCREENS: There appear to be sibling screens prod_tracking and sample_tracking (and an rfq page). Briefly compare: do licensing_tracking / prod_tracking / sample_tracking share a common grid "skin"/base and differ only in columns/endpoints? What is the relationship between rfq (the RFQ master grid) and licensing_tracking? Is licensing tracking essentially a costing/pricing-oriented grid, an approval-tracking grid, or both?

5. KEY FIELDS LIST: Provide the concrete list of column field names (the AG Grid colDef `field` values) for the licensing tracking grid, so they can be matched against another system's schema later.

Be concrete and exhaustive on the data model and fields — that is the most important part. Return your findings as a structured markdown report.

input.description: Deep-dive poppim-web PM system
input.prompt:
You are analyzing a project-management web application located at D:\repos\poppim-web (freshly cloned from github.com/u2giants/poppim-web). Produce a DETAILED, EVIDENCE-BACKED technical report. Cite file paths. Do NOT modify any files — read only.

Context: This app ("poppim") is a project-management system for a home-decor design company that runs two product lines — "POP Creations" (LICENSED products needing licensor approval, e.g. Disney/Marvel) and "Spruce Line" (generic/original). The nested `designflow-*` dirs don't exist in poppim-web (they were from a prior glob over `D:\repos\` — not part of this repo). Here is the report.

---

# Technical Report — poppim-web (POP Creations PIM)

## 1. Stack & Shape

**It is a frontend-only React SPA — a thin client of a shared Directus backend. It stores no data of its own and contains no schema, migrations, or Directus config.**

- **Framework / language:** React 19 + Vite 8 + TypeScript (strict, `noUnusedLocals`/`noUnusedParameters`). `package.json`: `"dev": "vite"`, `"build": "tsc -b && vite build"`.
- **UI:** Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui in the `new-york`/Radix style (`components.json`, `src/components/ui/*` are generated). `@dnd-kit/core` for board drag, `lucide-react` icons, `sonner` toasts, `next-themes`.
- **Backend access:** `@directus/sdk` v18 only. Client built in `src/lib/directus.ts` against `VITE_DIRECTUS_URL` (default hardcoded `https://data.designflow.app`). Auth is **session/cookie mode** (`authentication('session', { credentials:'include', autoRefresh:true })`) scoped to `.designflow.app`, enabling cross-subdomain Microsoft SSO via `GET {DIRECTUS_URL}/auth/login/microsoft?redirect=...`.
- **Hosting:** NOT Cloudflare Workers/D1. Built as a static SPA, containerized (`Dockerfile` multi-stage node→nginx, `nginx.conf` SPA fallback), pushed to `ghcr.io/u2giants/poppim-web`, deployed to **Coolify** (service uuid `ysvdyj3t7d5tyh5ogrvlka4y`) at `pm.designflow.app` via GitHub Actions on push to `main` (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, `docs/cicd.md`). Images for product covers live on **DigitalOcean Spaces** (`poppim.nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com/covers/`, `src/features/pipeline/adapter.ts`).

**Is Directus actually used?** Yes, as the runtime backend — every read/write goes through `@directus/sdk`. But **the Directus schema itself is not in this repo.** Per `AGENTS.md` §8 and `docs/architecture.md`, the collections/migrations live in a separate repo `u2giants/directus` (`pm-system/apply-schema.mjs`, `add-collaboration-model.mjs`). poppim-web only carries a **hand-maintained typed slice** of that schema in `src/lib/types.ts`. There is no `snapshot.yaml`, no SQL, no `directus.config`, no D1 here.

The backend Postgres is described (`docs/architecture.md`) as **shared across PIM + future CRM/DAM** — sibling frontends `popcrm-web` and `popdam-web` are separate repos. The `designflow-*` directories seen in an early glob are other repos under `D:\repos\`, not part of poppim-web.

## 2. Data Model / Schema

The schema poppim consumes is defined in `src/lib/types.ts` (the `Schema` interface, lines 173-188) plus native Directus `directus_users` and `directus_comments`. Collections and key fields:

**`product`** (the central entity — both "product/SKU" AND the pipeline "task"; there is no separate task table). Fields (`types.ts` 33-54):
`id, code, name, description, priority, business_unit, stage (M2O), retailer (M2O), licensor (M2O), project (M2O), on_shelf_date, pi_status, cover_url, clickup_url, clickup_list_name, clickup_created_at, clickup_updated_at, clickup_closed_at, clickup_start_at, clickup_due_at`. There are ~15,228 staged product rows (per `HANDOFF.md`).

**`project`** (the "offer"/collection grouping — 651 imported ClickUp records). Fields: `id, title, status, business_unit, retailer (M2O), on_shelf_date, brief, restrictions`.

**`stage`** (lifecycle/Kanban column). Fields: `id, name, stage_order, category, business_unit`.

**`retailer`** (buyer/customer): `id, name`.

**`licensor`** (the property/IP owner): `id, name`. Note: only `name` — no separate "property/style guide" entity.

**`stage`** is the only lifecycle model; product→stage is a single M2O pointer (`product.stage`). **There is no `stage_history` / status-transition log** — stage changes are a direct `updateItem('product', id, { stage })` (`board/api.ts setProductStage`).

**Collaboration collections** (added in the backend's `add-collaboration-model.mjs`):
- **`checklist_item`**: `id, product, label, done, sort, group_name, source_id, source_system`
- **`subtask`**: `id, product, title, done, assignee (M2O user), due_date, sort`
- **`product_assignee`** (M2M product↔directus_users): `id, product, directus_user`
- native **`directus_comments`** (filtered by `collection='product', item=<id>`)

**ClickUp-migration collections** (each M2O to `product`, surfaced in `TaskDetailModal`):
- **`product_file`**: `id, product, title, file_type, mime_type, size, source_url, thumbnail_url, stored_url, uploaded_at`
- **`product_update`**: `id, product, body, author_name, author_email, happened_at, kind`
- **`product_tag`**: `id, product, name, color`
- **`product_field`** (generic EAV for imported ClickUp custom fields): `id, product, name, field_type, value_text, value_json`
- **`product_activity`**: `id, product, action, detail, actor_name, happened_at`
- **`product_link`** (product↔product relations): `id, product, linked_product, linked_external_id, linked_title, relation_type, direction, created_by, created_at`
- **`product_time_entry`**: `id, product, user_name, user_email, started_at, ended_at, duration_ms, duration_hours, billable, description, tags`

**`directus_users`** (typed as `DirectusUser`): `id, first_name, last_name, email, avatar, role {id,name}`.

## 3. Licensing / Product Tracking

**poppim does NOT model a formal licensed-product approval process.** This is the most important finding. There is:

- **Licensor:** a flat `licensor` collection with only `id, name`. Display names are normalized client-side via a hardcoded map in `adapter.ts` (`LICENSOR_DISPLAY`: disney→Disney, marvel→Marvel, lucasfilm→Lucasfilm, sanrio, nbcu, wb, nick, peanuts, sega, wwe, care bears, coca cola, one piece, sesame street, strawberry shortcake, seasonal, star wars). `product.licensor` is the only product→licensor link.
- **No `property`, no `style_guide`, no `submission`, no `Brand Assurance`, no `PPS`/`sample` collection exists.** A grep across `src/` for `submission|brand assurance|PPS|approval|factory|vendor|costing|price|cost|style guide|property|sample|revision` returns only: (a) mock note prose in `mockData.ts` (e.g., "Licensor approval needed before sampling", "Factory hand-off"), and (b) unused optional fields `buyer?, factory?, retailer?, dueLicensor?` on the `MockTask` display interface (`mockData.ts` 90-93) that are populated on exactly one hardcoded mock card (t4) and never read from Directus.
- **Approval status is represented only as a `stage`.** The licensed-approval lifecycle is implied by stage *names* (mock `STAGES` = Concept → In Development → **Licensor Review** → **Approved** → Sampling → Production → Shipped, `mockData.ts:123`), but real stages come from the backend `stage` collection sorted by `stage_order`. There is no enum, no submission number, no approval-status field.
- **`pi_status`** (values "Required"/"Completed") is the only product-level status flag beyond stage; rendered as a "PI Req" pill (`adapter.ts piStatusPill`). "PI" appears to mean a packaging/product-information requirement, not licensor approval.
- **Product ↔ Project linkage:** `product.project` (M2O) is the parent-child relation. Projects (offers/collections) own many products; counted via a `groupBy:['project']` aggregate (`projects/api.ts`). `product_link` provides arbitrary product↔product relations (typed `relation_type, direction`), imported from ClickUp.
- **Business units:** `business_unit` exists as a free `string` field on `product`, `project`, and `stage` — this is where "POP Creations" (licensed) vs "Spruce Line" (generic) would be distinguished, but it is **not an enum and not a managed collection**; the UI only displays it as text on the Projects page and never filters POP vs Spruce. "Spruce" does not appear anywhere in the codebase.

**Bottom line:** licensing tracking in poppim today = `product.licensor` (name) + `product.stage` (a column whose name might say "Licensor Review"/"Approved") + free-text `business_unit`. There is no structured submission/approval/Brand-Assurance data model.

## 4. Features & State

**Live / wired to real Directus data:**
- **Pipeline (Kanban board)** — `src/features/pipeline/` — the substance of the app. Real `product` + `stage` data, `@dnd-kit` drag-to-change-stage with optimistic update + revert, server-side search (`name`/`code` `_icontains`) and licensor filter (`_in`), parallel `aggregate` count, debounced 300ms, "showing first N of M" truncation (loads 300 unfiltered / 500 filtered, not all 15K). Table view exists; **Timeline tab is a placeholder** (`AGENTS.md` §15 "open").
- **Projects page** — `src/features/projects/` — real 651 project records + per-project product counts + drill-in to a project's products.
- **Task Detail Modal** — `src/components/TaskDetailModal.tsx` — real assignees, checklist, native comments, plus four work panes (updates, files, fields, activity/links/time) all reading the `product_*` ClickUp-migration collections. Deep-linkable via `?item=<uuid>`.
- **Auth** — email/password + Microsoft SSO redirect; `App.tsx` is a simple auth gate (loading → LoginPage → AppShell). `react-router-dom` is installed but unused (no routes).
- **Cover images** on cards (DO Spaces thumbnails; full original in modal).

**Mock / scaffolding (static data from `src/lib/mockData.ts`, no API):**
- **My Work** (`mywork/`), **Schedule** (`schedule/`), **Notes** (`notes/`), **People** (`people/`), **Settings** (`settings/` — Licensors/Customers tabs with local-only logo upload). Sidebar "Teams" (Design/Sales/Licensing/Production) and "Collections" lists are hardcoded.

**Roles/permissions:** None defined in this repo. Roles are a Directus concept (`DirectusUser.role {id,name}` is read but never used for gating; the People page roles like "Licensing Manager" are mock strings). All permissions/roles live in the `directus` backend repo.

**No AI assistant, no flows/automations, no dashboards, no role-based views** exist in code.

**Completeness:** One genuinely functional module (Pipeline board + task detail + Projects) on real data; the other five nav screens are visual mocks. Per `HANDOFF.md`, production is deployed and SSO redirect verified but full real-tenant SSO round-trip unconfirmed. App is described as effectively preview-stage ("no production incidents — the app is preview-only so far").

## 5. Overlap Surface with DesignFlow PLM's licensing grid

A DesignFlow PLM grid tracking *licensor submissions, products/SKUs, approval status, sample/PPS status, Brand Assurance numbers, factory/vendor, costing/pricing per product* overlaps with poppim **only partially**, and poppim is the weaker model for licensing specifics:

| DesignFlow PLM field | Where it lives in poppim today | Mapping quality |
|---|---|---|
| Product / SKU | `product` collection (`code` = SKU-ish catalog code, `name`, `description`) | Direct — `product.code`/`product.name` |
| Licensor | `product.licensor` → `licensor.name` | Partial — name only, no property/style-guide |
| Property / style guide | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Approval status | implied by `product.stage.name` (e.g. "Licensor Review"/"Approved") | Weak — a free-form stage, not a status enum |
| Submission number | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Sample / PPS status | — (none; "Sampling" is just a possible stage name) | **No equivalent** |
| Brand Assurance number | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Factory / vendor | `MockTask.factory` (mock only) / possibly `product_field` EAV | **No real field** — would land in generic `product_field` |
| Costing / pricing | — (none; only mock note prose mentions price points) | **No equivalent** |
| Retailer / buyer | `product.retailer` → `retailer.name` | Direct |
| Project/offer grouping | `product.project` → `project` | Direct |
| Season | partial — `stage.category`, `business_unit`, "Seasonal" licensor | Weak/free-text |

**Where shared product/submission data would live in poppim:** the `product` row is the natural join key (by `code`/SKU or by `clickup_url`/`linked_external_id` on `product_link`). Any PLM licensing attributes not in poppim's typed schema (submission #, BA #, PPS status, factory, cost) would today only be representable through the generic **`product_field`** EAV collection (`name`/`value_text`/`value_json`) or imported **`product_tag`**s — i.e., unstructured. To make poppim a real licensing tracker matching the PLM grid, the **`directus` backend repo** would need new collections (submission, property/style_guide, factory/vendor, costing) or new `product` fields, and then `src/lib/types.ts` + the pipeline/modal UI extended to surface them. As-is, the concrete overlap is: **product/SKU, licensor (name), retailer/buyer, project grouping, and a coarse stage-as-status** — everything submission/PPS/BA/factory/costing-specific is absent from poppim's data model.

### Key files
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\types.ts` — the entire consumed schema slice
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\directus.ts` — SDK client, session auth, SSO URL
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\pipeline\api.ts`, `adapter.ts`, `PipelinePage.tsx` — board + filters + licensor normalization
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\board\api.ts`, `collab.ts` — stage updates + all collaboration/`product_*` queries
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\projects\api.ts` — project/offer grouping
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\components\TaskDetailModal.tsx` — work-data UI over `product_*` collections
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\mockData.ts` — mock screens + the only place "factory/buyer/price/PPS" wording appears
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\AGENTS.md` §8, `docs\architecture.md` — confirms schema lives in separate `u2giants/directus` repo

- **Approval status is represented only as a `stage`.** The licensed-approval lifecycle is implied by stage *names* (mock `STAGES` = Concept → In Development → **Licensor Review** → **Approved** → Sampling → Production → Shipped, `mockData.ts:123`), but real stages come from the backend `stage` collection sorted by `stage_order`. There is no enum, no submission number, no approval-status field.
- **`pi_status`** (values "Required"/"Completed") is the only product-level status flag beyond stage; rendered as a "PI Req" pill (`adapter.ts piStatusPill`). "PI" appears to mean a packaging/product-information requirement, not licensor approval.
- **Product ↔ Project linkage:** `product.project` (M2O) is the parent-child relation. Projects (offers/collections) own many products; counted via a `groupBy:['project']` aggregate (`projects/api.ts`). `product_link` provides arbitrary product↔product relations (typed `relation_type, direction`), imported from ClickUp.
- **Business units:** `business_unit` exists as a free `string` field on `product`, `project`, and `stage` — this is where "POP Creations" (licensed) vs "Spruce Line" (generic) would be distinguished, but it is **not an enum and not a managed collection**; the UI only displays it as text on the Projects page and never filters POP vs Spruce. "Spruce" does not appear anywhere in the codebase.

**Bottom line:** licensing tracking in poppim today = `product.licensor` (name) + `product.stage` (a column whose name might say "Licensor Review"/"Approved") + free-text `business_unit`. There is no structured submission/approval/Brand-Assurance data model.

## 4. Features & State

**Live / wired to real Directus data:**
- **Pipeline (Kanban board)** — `src/features/pipeline/` — the substance of the app. Real `product` + `stage` data, `@dnd-kit` drag-to-change-stage with optimistic update + revert, server-side search (`name`/`code` `_icontains`) and licensor filter (`_in`), parallel `aggregate` count, debounced 300ms, "showing first N of M" truncation (loads 300 unfiltered / 500 filtered, not all 15K). Table view exists; **Timeline tab is a placeholder** (`AGENTS.md` §15 "open").
- **Projects page** — `src/features/projects/` — real 651 project records + per-project product counts + drill-in to a project's products.
- **Task Detail Modal** — `src/components/TaskDetailModal.tsx` — real assignees, checklist, native comments, plus four work panes (updates, files, fields, activity/links/time) all reading the `product_*` ClickUp-migration collections. Deep-linkable via `?item=<uuid>`.
- **Auth** — email/password + Microsoft SSO redirect; `App.tsx` is a simple auth gate (loading → LoginPage → AppShell). `react-router-dom` is installed but unused (no routes).
- **Cover images** on cards (DO Spaces thumbnails; full original in modal).

**Mock / scaffolding (static data from `src/lib/mockData.ts`, no API):**
- **My Work** (`mywork/`), **Schedule** (`schedule/`), **Notes** (`notes/`), **People** (`people/`), **Settings** (`settings/` — Licensors/Customers tabs with local-only logo upload). Sidebar "Teams" (Design/Sales/Licensing/Production) and "Collections" lists are hardcoded.

**Roles/permissions:** None defined in this repo. Roles are a Directus concept (`DirectusUser.role {id,name}` is read but never used for gating; the People page roles like "Licensing Manager" are mock strings). All permissions/roles live in the `directus` backend repo.

**No AI assistant, no flows/automations, no dashboards, no role-based views** exist in code.

**Completeness:** One genuinely functional module (Pipeline board + task detail + Projects) on real data; the other five nav screens are visual mocks. Per `HANDOFF.md`, production is deployed and SSO redirect verified but full real-tenant SSO round-trip unconfirmed. App is described as effectively preview-stage ("no production incidents — the app is preview-only so far").

## 5. Overlap Surface with DesignFlow PLM's licensing grid

A DesignFlow PLM grid tracking *licensor submissions, products/SKUs, approval status, sample/PPS status, Brand Assurance numbers, factory/vendor, costing/pricing per product* overlaps with poppim **only partially**, and poppim is the weaker model for licensing specifics:

| DesignFlow PLM field | Where it lives in poppim today | Mapping quality |
|---|---|---|
| Product / SKU | `product` collection (`code` = SKU-ish catalog code, `name`, `description`) | Direct — `product.code`/`product.name` |
| Licensor | `product.licensor` → `licensor.name` | Partial — name only, no property/style-guide |
| Property / style guide | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Approval status | implied by `product.stage.name` (e.g. "Licensor Review"/"Approved") | Weak — a free-form stage, not a status enum |
| Submission number | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Sample / PPS status | — (none; "Sampling" is just a possible stage name) | **No equivalent** |
| Brand Assurance number | — (none) | **No equivalent** |
| Factory / vendor | `MockTask.factory` (mock only) / possibly `product_field` EAV | **No real field** — would land in generic `product_field` |
| Costing / pricing | — (none; only mock note prose mentions price points) | **No equivalent** |
| Retailer / buyer | `product.retailer` → `retailer.name` | Direct |
| Project/offer grouping | `product.project` → `project` | Direct |
| Season | partial — `stage.category`, `business_unit`, "Seasonal" licensor | Weak/free-text |

**Where shared product/submission data would live in poppim:** the `product` row is the natural join key (by `code`/SKU or by `clickup_url`/`linked_external_id` on `product_link`). Any PLM licensing attributes not in poppim's typed schema (submission #, BA #, PPS status, factory, cost) would today only be representable through the generic **`product_field`** EAV collection (`name`/`value_text`/`value_json`) or imported **`product_tag`**s — i.e., unstructured. To make poppim a real licensing tracker matching the PLM grid, the **`directus` backend repo** would need new collections (submission, property/style_guide, factory/vendor, costing) or new `product` fields, and then `src/lib/types.ts` + the pipeline/modal UI extended to surface them. As-is, the concrete overlap is: **product/SKU, licensor (name), retailer/buyer, project grouping, and a coarse stage-as-status** — everything submission/PPS/BA/factory/costing-specific is absent from poppim's data model.

### Key files
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\types.ts` — the entire consumed schema slice
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\directus.ts` — SDK client, session auth, SSO URL
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\pipeline\api.ts`, `adapter.ts`, `PipelinePage.tsx` — board + filters + licensor normalization
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\board\api.ts`, `collab.ts` — stage updates + all collaboration/`product_*` queries
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\features\projects\api.ts` — project/offer grouping
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\components\TaskDetailModal.tsx` — work-data UI over `product_*` collections
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\src\lib\mockData.ts` — mock screens + the only place "factory/buyer/price/PPS" wording appears
- `D:\repos\poppim-web\AGENTS.md` §8, `docs\architecture.md` — confirms schema lives in separate `u2giants/directus` repo

content.text:
I have all the evidence needed. Here is the full report.

---

# Licensing Tracking Module — Technical Report (DesignFlow PLM)

## 0. Files examined (evidence base)

| Layer | File |
|---|---|
| Grid column config | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/license.tracking.config.ts` |
| Component logic | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/licensing_tracking.component.ts` |
| Template | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/licensing_tracking.component.html` |
| Grid-data API service | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/licensing_tracking.service.ts` |
| Workflow API service | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/item_detail.service.ts` (lines 84–110) |
| Feedback (approval) dialog | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/license-tracking/feedback_dialog/feedback_dialog.component.ts` |
| Tracking route | `designflow-tracking/routes/lic.router.js` |
| Tracking controller | `designflow-tracking/controllers/lic.controller.js` |
| Tracking model (the SQL) | `designflow-tracking/models/lic.model.js` |
| Row remap / write map | `designflow-tracking/helpers/utility.js` (`remapLicItem` @1452, `mapLicItem` @1534) |
| DB models | `designflow-tracking/models/db/{itemHeader,licensingStatus,LicenseFeedBacks,licensingMilestone,itemLicenseImage,itemAttachment,merchGroup,externalVendor}.js` |
| Workflow seed (backend) | `designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js` (`getLicenseFeedBacks` @3892, reads `licenseList`) |

---

## 1. Purpose & UI

The licensing tracking screen is an **AG Grid Enterprise server-side, paginated, master/detail grid** that shows **one row per licensed item/product (an `itemHeader` record = a SKU/item)** — *not* one row per licensor submission and not per style number. Confirmed by `lic.model.js:158` (`sql.itemHeader.findAndCountAll`), ordered by `item_id_pk DESC`, and `getRowId` keyed on `params.data.id` = `item_id_pk` (`licensing_tracking.component.ts:246`, remap `'id': item.dataValues.item_id_pk` at `utility.js:1456`).

**Grid scope filter:** a "Product Type" toolbar dropdown toggles between **Licensed** (`divisionCodes [1,8]`, and code 1 implicitly adds 2 — `lic.model.js:123`) and **Generic** (`divisionCodes [9]`) — `licensing_tracking.component.ts:113-117, 371-375`.

**Columns actually rendered** (only 13 — see §5), grouped:
- **Identity:** Picture (image), Item Number (`item_num_id`, read-only), Item Desc.
- **Licensor / property:** Licensor Code (resolved from merchGroup type `05`), Artist (merchGroup type `09`), For Customer (`udf_merchgroup15_fk`), season.
- **Dates:** On Shelf Date (`udf_date01`), Due Date, Updated On (`lic_tracking_updated_date`).
- **Status/stage:** License Status (derived from the latest `licensingStatus` row).
- **Compliance:** Brand Assurance Number (`udf_freeform_03`, read-only).
- **Sample/PPS:** Sample No (`lic_sample_no`).
- **Free text:** Comment.
- **Pricing / factory / vendor:** **none rendered in the default grid** (see §4 — they exist in the schema and in division-toggle logic but are not in `license.tracking.config.ts`).

**User actions:**
- **Inline cell edits** → `onCellValueChanged` POSTs `/lic/updateLic` and writes an audit log (`saveLog`, module `licensing_tracking`).
- **License Status dropdown** (`agSelectCellEditor`, values from `/lic/statuses`).
- **Double-click any cell** (except License Status) → navigates to `/apps/itemDetail/{id}?tab=licensing` (`component.ts:200-209`).
- **Right-click context menu:** "Add feedback" (single row → `FeedbackDialogComponent`; multi-row → `BulkUpdateComponent` with License/Package → From → POP/Licensor → phase|status submenu), Archive/Unarchive, cell notes, copy, export, and (admin) "Recalculate with new Freight", plus a "Duty With $ and %" dialog (`DutyRateDialogComponent`) — note the duty/freight menu items only fire if a `fob_cost` field exists, which is dormant in this grid.
- **Image upload / history** via `imageEditor` cell renderer + `/lic/images/:itemId` endpoints.
- **Excel export** (with embedded cell images), **grid views** (save/load/default per-user, `grid_id: 'licensingTracking'`), column-layout dropdown, reset-filters, active-filter chips.

---

## 2. Data Model

### Two backends serve this one screen

**(A) Grid data + inline edits → tracking backend (port 5002), `environment.tracking`, `/lic/*`:**

| Frontend service method | HTTP | Route (`lic.router.js`) → controller → model |
|---|---|---|
| `getAllLicenses()` | GET `/lic/getLic` | `getAllLicenses` → `LicTracking.getAllLicenses` (`lic.model.js:19`) |
| `updateProdOrderHeader()` | POST `/lic/updateLic` | `updateLic` → `itemHeader.update` via `mapLicItem` |
| `getLicenseStatuses()` | GET `/lic/statuses` | reads distinct `LicenseFeedBacks.status` |
| `getSelectedGridView()` / `getViewlayout()` | GET | `GridAccessLevel` + `GridLayout` |
| `getAllUsers / groups CRUD` | various | `users`, `groups` tables |
| image history | GET/POST `/lic/images*` | `itemAttachment` table |

**(B) Approval-workflow actions → main backend (port 5000), `item_detail.service.ts`:**

| Frontend method | Endpoint (backend) | Backing data |
|---|---|---|
| `getLicenseFeedBacks({from})` | `/getLicenseFeedBacks` | `Customer.getLicenseFeedBacks` (`lib.model.js:3892`) reads **`licenseList`** (status `ACTIVE`) — the catalog of phase/status options |
| `AddLicensingStatus` / `BulkAddLicensingStatus` / `BulkGetLicensingStatus` / `DeleteLicensingStatus` | `/AddLicensingStatus` etc. | writes **`licensingStatus`** rows (the per-item event log) |

Both backends point at the same PostgreSQL schema (`process.env.SCHEMA`), so `licensingStatus` written by backend (5000) is read by tracking (5002) in `getAllLicenses`.

### Core query (`lic.model.js:158-187`)
`itemHeader.findAndCountAll` with `include`:
- `externalVendor` (as `externalVendor`)
- `itemAttachment` (as `itemAttachments`) — images
- `itemLicenseImage` (as `licenseImages`)
- `comments`
- **`licensingStatus`** (as `licensingStatuses`) — filtered to the **latest row per item** via a `MAX(id)` correlated subquery; this drives the License Status column and the feedback "history".
- `merchGroup` joins (`merchGroup01..06, 09`) for licensor/artist/merch-group resolution.

### Actual tables / models and key licensing columns

**`itemHeader`** (`tableName: 'itemHeader'`, PK `item_id_pk`) — the master row. Relevant columns:
- Identity: `item_num_id`, `item_displ_descr_name`, `div_code_fk`, `compan_code_fk`, `season_code_fk`.
- Licensor/property (UDF-encoded): `udf_merchgroup05_fk` (+`_id`) = licensor, `udf_merchgroup09_fk` (+`_id`) = artist, `udf_merchgroup15_fk` = for-customer, `udf_freeform_03` = Brand Assurance #, `udf_date01` = on-shelf date.
- **A full dormant licensing-workflow column block** (`itemHeader.js:731-866`), most of which the grid never shows: `lic_comment, lic_compnay, lic_sample_no, lic_item_desc, lic_licensorcode, lic_concept_submiteed(+_date), due_date, lic_concept_rejected(+_date), lic_concept_approved(+_date), lic_sample_requested(+_date), lic_sample_made(+_date), lic_vendor_sent(+_date), lic_office_received(+_date), lic_office_sent(+_date), lic_dev_received / lic_dev_sample_recv_date, lic_dev_sample_sent(+_date), lic_prepo_approved(+_date), lic_prepo_rejected(+_date), lic_brand_assurance_number, lic_order_placed(+_date), lic_tracking_updated_date`. These are written by `mapLicItem` (`utility.js:1534`) but **not surfaced in the current grid** — they are a legacy/alternate flat-column model of the same workflow now superseded by the `licensingStatus` event table.

**`licensingStatus`** (`tableName:'licensingStatus'`, no timestamps) — **the per-item licensing event/feedback log** (the live workflow record):
`id, date(string), feedback, attachments(JSONB), package(bool), status, from, moduser, itemheader_id_fk, assignor_id, assignee_id, pop_comments, tagged_group_id(→groups.id)`.

**`LicenseFeedBacks`** (`tableName:'LicenseFeedBacks'`) — **the catalog of valid workflow phases/statuses**: `id, phase, status, explanation, duplicable(bool), order, item_order`. Read by `/lic/statuses` (tracking) and mirrored by `licenseList` on the backend for `getLicenseFeedBacks`.

**`licensingMilestone`** (`tableName:'licensingMilestone'`, timestamps) — `itemheader_id_fk, stage, milestone_date, checked_by_user_id, checked_by_name`. Defined but **not referenced** by the licensing grid query (appears unused by this screen).

**`itemLicenseImage`** — `itemheader_id_fk, phase, image_link, thumb_link, created_user, created_time`. **`itemAttachment`** — generic image/file store actually used for the Picture column.

**`merchGroup`** — `mgTypeCode` (`05`=licensor, `09`=artist), `mg_code`, `mg_desc`, `divisionCode_id_fk`; resolves codes to display names in `remapLicItem`.

---

## 3. Workflow Mapping (licensor approval process)

The vocabulary of statuses (from `FeedbackDialogComponent.onSelectFrom`, lines 189-206) and the `LicenseFeedBacks` catalog establishes which business steps are modeled:

| Business step | Captured? | Where |
|---|---|---|
| Brief → preliminary design → buyer pick | **No** | Not in this module (upstream RFQ/item-library) |
| SKU creation | **Implicit** | The row *is* the SKU (`itemHeader`) |
| Licensing sheet / Liz internal review | **Partial** | Status `Ready to Submit` (designers restricted to this), `from: POP` internal feedback, assignee + tagged group routing |
| Concept submitted to licensor | **Yes** | Status `Concept Submitted`, `Packaging Concept Submitted` (`from: Licensor`) |
| Licensor responds (approve/revise/reject) | **Yes** | `Concept Not Approved at All`, plus approve/reject statuses; legacy `lic_concept_approved/rejected` columns |
| PO / sample request | **Yes** | `Request Pre-Pro Sample`; legacy `lic_sample_requested`, `lic_order_placed` |
| Factory sample (PPS) made / shipped | **Partial** | `Sample No` column + legacy `lic_sample_made`, `lic_vendor_sent`, `lic_office_received/sent`, `lic_dev_*` columns (event-level dates exist but aren't in grid) |
| PPS submitted to licensor | **Yes** | `Pre-Pro Submitted` |
| PPS approved | **Yes** | `Pre-pro Approved` |
| Mass production / production approved-closed | **No** (in licensing) | Lives in **prod_tracking** (`ProdOrderHeader`/`Detail`) |
| Shipping / **Brand Assurance form** | **Partial** | `Brand Assurance Number` field captured (`udf_freeform_03`); the BA *form/shipping* itself is not generated here |

**Net:** The screen captures the **licensor-facing approval loop** (internal review → concept submission → licensor response → pre-pro sample request → PPS submission → PPS approval) as a per-item event log (`licensingStatus`), plus a Brand Assurance reference number. It does **not** capture the upstream brief/design/buyer-pick stages, nor the downstream mass-production execution and physical shipping/BA-form generation. The "Package vs License" toggle in the feedback dialog means each item is tracked along **two parallel approval tracks** (artwork/concept and packaging).

---

## 4. Relationship to sibling screens

**Shared "skin" / base.** licensing_tracking, prod_tracking, and sample_tracking are three instances of **one common AG Grid pattern**, not a shared base class — each has its own component but they were cloned from the same factory:
- All three column configs use the identical `addColumn(...) + commonDefaults` factory (`license.tracking.config.ts`, `sample.tracking.config.ts` whose header comment explicitly says *"Mirrors the addColumn / commonDefaults factory used by the other tracking grids (e.g. license.tracking.config.ts)"*, and `prod.tracking.detail.config.ts`).
- licensing_tracking and prod_tracking **share the same write endpoint**: both call `LicensingTrackingService.updateProdOrderHeader()` → `/lic/updateLic` is the licensing one; prod_tracking calls the same-named service method (`prod_tracking.component.ts:1542`) but **reads** via `trackingService.getProdOrderHeader()` (line 1183) against `ProdOrderHeader`. So they differ by **read endpoint + column config + underlying table** (`itemHeader` vs `ProdOrderHeader`), sharing the grid framework, view/layout system, cell-notes, and image/export plumbing.
- sample_tracking is the newest sibling (its own thin tables `sample`, `sample_event`, etc. per the config), reusing only the visual skin.

**RFQ relationship.** The licensing grid is **structurally the RFQ grid's sibling and partial clone**: the component imports RFQ sub-components (`rfqDatepickerComponent`, `rfqAutocompleteComponent`, `rfq/custom-tooltip`), its context menu calls `archivedRFQItem({url:'rfq'})`, `updateRfqItem`, `recalculate_Lic_Gen`, and references RFQ pricing fields (`fob_cost`, `whsecost`, `duty_rate`, `lic_fob_royalty`, `lic_*_sell_price/margin/price_sales`, `gen_*` equivalents) in `onDivisionCodeChange` and the duty/freight menus. RFQ is the **costing/pricing master grid**; licensing_tracking reuses that costing machinery but **its default visible columns are approval-tracking, not pricing**.

**Is licensing tracking costing or approval?** **Both by lineage, approval by current use.** The code carries a full RFQ-style costing apparatus (duty, freight recalculation, royalty/sell-price column show/hide per division, equation engine `recalculate_Lic_Gen`), but the **rendered default grid is purely approval/status-tracking** (status, licensor, artist, dates, BA#, sample#). The pricing columns exist in the schema and toggle logic but are **not in `license.tracking.config.ts`**, so in practice this is an **approval-tracking grid built on the RFQ costing chassis**.

---

## 5. Key field list (AG Grid `field` / `colId` for the licensing grid)

From `license.tracking.config.ts` (the `field` value is the display header; `colId` is the stable id — note this grid uses display names as `field` and remaps server-side via `mapFieldToColumn`):

| `field` (header) | `colId` | DB column (`mapFieldToColumn` / `remapLicItem`) | editable |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Picture` | `pic` | `itemAttachments[].attachment_link` (+`FullSizePicture`) | no (image editor) |
| `License Status` | `license_status` | derived: latest `licensingStatus.status` | yes (select) |
| `Item Number` | `item_num_id` | `item_num_id` | no |
| `Item Desc` | `item_desc` | `item_displ_descr_name` | yes |
| `Licensor Code` | `licensorCode` | `udf_merchgroup05_fk` → `merchGroup(05).mg_desc` | no |
| `For Customer` | `for_cust` | `udf_merchgroup15_fk` | yes |
| `Artist` | `artist` | `udf_merchgroup09_fk` → `merchGroup(09).mg_desc` | yes |
| `On Shelf Date` | `on_shelf_date` | `udf_date01` | no (date) |
| `Due Date` | `due_date` | `due_date` | no (date) |
| `Updated On` | `updated_date` | `lic_tracking_updated_date` | no (date) |
| `season` | `season` | `season_code_fk` | yes |
| `Brand Assurance Number` | `brand_assurance_number` | `udf_freeform_03` | no |
| `Sample No` | `sample_no` | `lic_sample_no` | yes |
| `Comment` | `comment` | latest `comments[].comment` else `lic_comment` | yes |

Hidden payload fields also returned per row (useful for matching another schema): `id` (=`item_id_pk`), `FullSizePicture`, `divcode` (`div_code_fk`), and `history[]` = array of `licensingStatus` rows (`{id, date, status, feedback, from, user, pkg}`).

**Server-side filterable field → column map** (`mapFieldToColumn`, `lic.model.js:222`): `item_num_id→item_num_id`, `item_desc→item_displ_descr_name`, `brand_assurance_number→udf_freeform_03`, `licensorCode→merchGroup05.udf_merchgroup05_fk_id`, `comment→lic_comment`, `season→season_code_fk`, `for_cust→udf_merchgroup15_fk`, `artist→merchGroup09.udf_merchgroup09_fk_id`, `sample_no→lic_sample_no`, `due_date→due_date`, `updated_date→lic_tracking_updated_date`, `on_shelf_date→udf_date01`.

---

## Key findings / caveats

1. **The row entity is a SKU/item (`itemHeader`)**, not a licensor submission. Submissions are child events in `licensingStatus`, surfaced only as the single latest status + a `history[]` array.
2. **Two backends, one schema:** grid reads/writes go to tracking-5002 `/lic/*`; the approval-feedback create/read goes to backend-5000 (`AddLicensingStatus`, `getLicenseFeedBacks`/`licenseList`). They share the same Postgres tables, so this split is purely a routing artifact.
3. **Large dormant schema:** `itemHeader` has ~40 `lic_*` workflow columns (concept/sample/prepo/order milestones) written by `mapLicItem` but absent from the visible grid — the live workflow has migrated to the `licensingStatus` event table. `licensingMilestone` appears entirely unused by this screen.
4. **Costing chassis, approval skin:** the component inherits RFQ pricing/duty/freight/royalty logic (division-based column show/hide, `recalculate_Lic_Gen`, duty dialog) but ships **no pricing columns** in the default config — those code paths are largely dead for this grid as configured.
5. **For schema-matching another system**, the 13 `colId`s in §5 plus the `mapFieldToColumn` table give the exact field-to-DB-column correspondence; the licensor/artist/customer fields are UDF-encoded merchGroup foreign keys (types `05`/`09`/`15`), and Brand Assurance # is `udf_freeform_03`.

Code-quality note worth flagging separately: `utility.js` lines 1463 and 1550 contain stray leading backslashes (`\ [colHeaders.dlvy_loc]` and `\sample_aprov_lead_day`) and `remapLicItem` reads `item.dataValues.deliveryLocations`/`company` which are not in the `getAllLicenses` include list — potential latent bugs, but outside the scope of this read-only report.
