toolUseResult.prompt:
You are analyzing the "licensing tracking" feature of the DesignFlow PLM application (a multi-repo Angular + Node/Express + PostgreSQL system). Produce a DETAILED, EVIDENCE-BACKED technical report. Cite file paths (and line numbers where useful). Do NOT modify any files — read only.

Repos (all on D:\repos\dflow\):
- designflow-frontend (Angular 21, AG Grid Enterprise) — port 4200
- designflow-tracking (Node/Express + PostgreSQL + Sequelize) — port 5002
- designflow-backend (Node/Express + PostgreSQL + Sequelize) — port 5000
- designflow-item-master (5003), designflow-bff (5004 proxy), designflow-data-syncing (5001)

PRIMARY TARGET: the licensing tracking module at
  designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/
especially licensing_tracking.component.ts, .html, the module, and its AG Grid column config (search designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/ for a licensing-tracking grid config).

Investigate and report on:

1. PURPOSE & UI: What does the licensing tracking screen actually do? What is the grid showing — one row per what business object (a licensor submission? a product/SKU? a style number)? What columns/fields exist (list the meaningful ones, grouped: identity, licensor/property, dates, status/stage, sample/PPS, compliance like Brand Assurance / PI, pricing, factory/vendor, files/images)? What actions can a user take (status changes, image upload, vendor assignment, dialogs, exports)?

2. DATA MODEL: Which backend serves this screen? Trace the API service methods it calls (look in designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts and any other service) and map them to backend endpoints/controllers/routes and Sequelize models/DB tables (search designflow-tracking and designflow-backend models/). List the actual table/model names and their key columns relevant to licensing. Note the licensor submission / Brand Assurance / sample / approval fields if present.

3. WORKFLOW MAPPING: Map what this screen tracks to the real licensor approval business process: brief → preliminary design → buyer pick → SKU → licensing sheet → Liz internal review → concept submitted to licensor → licensor responds (approve/revise) → PO or sample request → factory sample (PPS) → internal review → PPS submitted to licensor → PPS approved → mass production → production approved/closed → shipping (Brand Assurance form). Which of these steps does the DesignFlow licensing tracking screen actually capture, and which does it NOT capture?

4. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER TRACKING SCREENS: There appear to be sibling screens prod_tracking and sample_tracking (and an rfq page). Briefly compare: do licensing_tracking / prod_tracking / sample_tracking share a common grid "skin"/base and differ only in columns/endpoints? What is the relationship between rfq (the RFQ master grid) and licensing_tracking? Is licensing tracking essentially a costing/pricing-oriented grid, an approval-tracking grid, or both?

5. KEY FIELDS LIST: Provide the concrete list of column field names (the AG Grid colDef `field` values) for the licensing tracking grid, so they can be matched against another system's schema later.

Be concrete and exhaustive on the data model and fields — that is the most important part. Return your findings as a structured markdown report.

content.text:
I have all the evidence needed. Here is the full report.

---

# Licensing Tracking Module — Technical Report (DesignFlow PLM)

## 0. Files examined (evidence base)

| Layer | File |
|---|---|
| Grid column config | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/license.tracking.config.ts` |
| Component logic | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/licensing_tracking.component.ts` |
| Template | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/licensing_tracking.component.html` |
| Grid-data API service | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/licensing_tracking.service.ts` |
| Workflow API service | `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/item_detail.service.ts` (lines 84–110) |
| Feedback (approval) dialog | `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/license-tracking/feedback_dialog/feedback_dialog.component.ts` |
| Tracking route | `designflow-tracking/routes/lic.router.js` |
| Tracking controller | `designflow-tracking/controllers/lic.controller.js` |
| Tracking model (the SQL) | `designflow-tracking/models/lic.model.js` |
| Row remap / write map | `designflow-tracking/helpers/utility.js` (`remapLicItem` @1452, `mapLicItem` @1534) |
| DB models | `designflow-tracking/models/db/{itemHeader,licensingStatus,LicenseFeedBacks,licensingMilestone,itemLicenseImage,itemAttachment,merchGroup,externalVendor}.js` |
| Workflow seed (backend) | `designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js` (`getLicenseFeedBacks` @3892, reads `licenseList`) |

---

## 1. Purpose & UI

The licensing tracking screen is an **AG Grid Enterprise server-side, paginated, master/detail grid** that shows **one row per licensed item/product (an `itemHeader` record = a SKU/item)** — *not* one row per licensor submission and not per style number. Confirmed by `lic.model.js:158` (`sql.itemHeader.findAndCountAll`), ordered by `item_id_pk DESC`, and `getRowId` keyed on `params.data.id` = `item_id_pk` (`licensing_tracking.component.ts:246`, remap `'id': item.dataValues.item_id_pk` at `utility.js:1456`).

**Grid scope filter:** a "Product Type" toolbar dropdown toggles between **Licensed** (`divisionCodes [1,8]`, and code 1 implicitly adds 2 — `lic.model.js:123`) and **Generic** (`divisionCodes [9]`) — `licensing_tracking.component.ts:113-117, 371-375`.

**Columns actually rendered** (only 13 — see §5), grouped:
- **Identity:** Picture (image), Item Number (`item_num_id`, read-only), Item Desc.
- **Licensor / property:** Licensor Code (resolved from merchGroup type `05`), Artist (merchGroup type `09`), For Customer (`udf_merchgroup15_fk`), season.
- **Dates:** On Shelf Date (`udf_date01`), Due Date, Updated On (`lic_tracking_updated_date`).
- **Status/stage:** License Status (derived from the latest `licensingStatus` row).
- **Compliance:** Brand Assurance Number (`udf_freeform_03`, read-only).
- **Sample/PPS:** Sample No (`lic_sample_no`).
- **Free text:** Comment.
- **Pricing / factory / vendor:** **none rendered in the default grid** (see §4 — they exist in the schema and in division-toggle logic but are not in `license.tracking.config.ts`).

**User actions:**
- **Inline cell edits** → `onCellValueChanged` POSTs `/lic/updateLic` and writes an audit log (`saveLog`, module `licensing_tracking`).
- **License Status dropdown** (`agSelectCellEditor`, values from `/lic/statuses`).
- **Double-click any cell** (except License Status) → navigates to `/apps/itemDetail/{id}?tab=licensing` (`component.ts:200-209`).
- **Right-click context menu:** "Add feedback" (single row → `FeedbackDialogComponent`; multi-row → `BulkUpdateComponent` with License/Package → From → POP/Licensor → phase|status submenu), Archive/Unarchive, cell notes, copy, export, and (admin) "Recalculate with new Freight", plus a "Duty With $ and %" dialog (`DutyRateDialogComponent`) — note the duty/freight menu items only fire if a `fob_cost` field exists, which is dormant in this grid.
- **Image upload / history** via `imageEditor` cell renderer + `/lic/images/:itemId` endpoints.
- **Excel export** (with embedded cell images), **grid views** (save/load/default per-user, `grid_id: 'licensingTracking'`), column-layout dropdown, reset-filters, active-filter chips.

---

## 2. Data Model

### Two backends serve this one screen

**(A) Grid data + inline edits → tracking backend (port 5002), `environment.tracking`, `/lic/*`:**

| Frontend service method | HTTP | Route (`lic.router.js`) → controller → model |
|---|---|---|
| `getAllLicenses()` | GET `/lic/getLic` | `getAllLicenses` → `LicTracking.getAllLicenses` (`lic.model.js:19`) |
| `updateProdOrderHeader()` | POST `/lic/updateLic` | `updateLic` → `itemHeader.update` via `mapLicItem` |
| `getLicenseStatuses()` | GET `/lic/statuses` | reads distinct `LicenseFeedBacks.status` |
| `getSelectedGridView()` / `getViewlayout()` | GET | `GridAccessLevel` + `GridLayout` |
| `getAllUsers / groups CRUD` | various | `users`, `groups` tables |
| image history | GET/POST `/lic/images*` | `itemAttachment` table |

**(B) Approval-workflow actions → main backend (port 5000), `item_detail.service.ts`:**

| Frontend method | Endpoint (backend) | Backing data |
|---|---|---|
| `getLicenseFeedBacks({from})` | `/getLicenseFeedBacks` | `Customer.getLicenseFeedBacks` (`lib.model.js:3892`) reads **`licenseList`** (status `ACTIVE`) — the catalog of phase/status options |
| `AddLicensingStatus` / `BulkAddLicensingStatus` / `BulkGetLicensingStatus` / `DeleteLicensingStatus` | `/AddLicensingStatus` etc. | writes **`licensingStatus`** rows (the per-item event log) |

Both backends point at the same PostgreSQL schema (`process.env.SCHEMA`), so `licensingStatus` written by backend (5000) is read by tracking (5002) in `getAllLicenses`.

### Core query (`lic.model.js:158-187`)
`itemHeader.findAndCountAll` with `include`:
- `externalVendor` (as `externalVendor`)
- `itemAttachment` (as `itemAttachments`) — images
- `itemLicenseImage` (as `licenseImages`)
- `comments`
- **`licensingStatus`** (as `licensingStatuses`) — filtered to the **latest row per item** via a `MAX(id)` correlated subquery; this drives the License Status column and the feedback "history".
- `merchGroup` joins (`merchGroup01..06, 09`) for licensor/artist/merch-group resolution.

### Actual tables / models and key licensing columns

**`itemHeader`** (`tableName: 'itemHeader'`, PK `item_id_pk`) — the master row. Relevant columns:
- Identity: `item_num_id`, `item_displ_descr_name`, `div_code_fk`, `compan_code_fk`, `season_code_fk`.
- Licensor/property (UDF-encoded): `udf_merchgroup05_fk` (+`_id`) = licensor, `udf_merchgroup09_fk` (+`_id`) = artist, `udf_merchgroup15_fk` = for-customer, `udf_freeform_03` = Brand Assurance #, `udf_date01` = on-shelf date.
- **A full dormant licensing-workflow column block** (`itemHeader.js:731-866`), most of which the grid never shows: `lic_comment, lic_compnay, lic_sample_no, lic_item_desc, lic_licensorcode, lic_concept_submiteed(+_date), due_date, lic_concept_rejected(+_date), lic_concept_approved(+_date), lic_sample_requested(+_date), lic_sample_made(+_date), lic_vendor_sent(+_date), lic_office_received(+_date), lic_office_sent(+_date), lic_dev_received / lic_dev_sample_recv_date, lic_dev_sample_sent(+_date), lic_prepo_approved(+_date), lic_prepo_rejected(+_date), lic_brand_assurance_number, lic_order_placed(+_date), lic_tracking_updated_date`. These are written by `mapLicItem` (`utility.js:1534`) but **not surfaced in the current grid** — they are a legacy/alternate flat-column model of the same workflow now superseded by the `licensingStatus` event table.

**`licensingStatus`** (`tableName:'licensingStatus'`, no timestamps) — **the per-item licensing event/feedback log** (the live workflow record):
`id, date(string), feedback, attachments(JSONB), package(bool), status, from, moduser, itemheader_id_fk, assignor_id, assignee_id, pop_comments, tagged_group_id(→groups.id)`.

**`LicenseFeedBacks`** (`tableName:'LicenseFeedBacks'`) — **the catalog of valid workflow phases/statuses**: `id, phase, status, explanation, duplicable(bool), order, item_order`. Read by `/lic/statuses` (tracking) and mirrored by `licenseList` on the backend for `getLicenseFeedBacks`.

**`licensingMilestone`** (`tableName:'licensingMilestone'`, timestamps) — `itemheader_id_fk, stage, milestone_date, checked_by_user_id, checked_by_name`. Defined but **not referenced** by the licensing grid query (appears unused by this screen).

**`itemLicenseImage`** — `itemheader_id_fk, phase, image_link, thumb_link, created_user, created_time`. **`itemAttachment`** — generic image/file store actually used for the Picture column.

**`merchGroup`** — `mgTypeCode` (`05`=licensor, `09`=artist), `mg_code`, `mg_desc`, `divisionCode_id_fk`; resolves codes to display names in `remapLicItem`.

---

## 3. Workflow Mapping (licensor approval process)

The vocabulary of statuses (from `FeedbackDialogComponent.onSelectFrom`, lines 189-206) and the `LicenseFeedBacks` catalog establishes which business steps are modeled:

| Business step | Captured? | Where |
|---|---|---|
| Brief → preliminary design → buyer pick | **No** | Not in this module (upstream RFQ/item-library) |
| SKU creation | **Implicit** | The row *is* the SKU (`itemHeader`) |
| Licensing sheet / Liz internal review | **Partial** | Status `Ready to Submit` (designers restricted to this), `from: POP` internal feedback, assignee + tagged group routing |
| Concept submitted to licensor | **Yes** | Status `Concept Submitted`, `Packaging Concept Submitted` (`from: Licensor`) |
| Licensor responds (approve/revise/reject) | **Yes** | `Concept Not Approved at All`, plus approve/reject statuses; legacy `lic_concept_approved/rejected` columns |
| PO / sample request | **Yes** | `Request Pre-Pro Sample`; legacy `lic_sample_requested`, `lic_order_placed` |
| Factory sample (PPS) made / shipped | **Partial** | `Sample No` column + legacy `lic_sample_made`, `lic_vendor_sent`, `lic_office_received/sent`, `lic_dev_*` columns (event-level dates exist but aren't in grid) |
| PPS submitted to licensor | **Yes** | `Pre-Pro Submitted` |
| PPS approved | **Yes** | `Pre-pro Approved` |
| Mass production / production approved-closed | **No** (in licensing) | Lives in **prod_tracking** (`ProdOrderHeader`/`Detail`) |
| Shipping / **Brand Assurance form** | **Partial** | `Brand Assurance Number` field captured (`udf_freeform_03`); the BA *form/shipping* itself is not generated here |

**Net:** The screen captures the **licensor-facing approval loop** (internal review → concept submission → licensor response → pre-pro sample request → PPS submission → PPS approval) as a per-item event log (`licensingStatus`), plus a Brand Assurance reference number. It does **not** capture the upstream brief/design/buyer-pick stages, nor the downstream mass-production execution and physical shipping/BA-form generation. The "Package vs License" toggle in the feedback dialog means each item is tracked along **two parallel approval tracks** (artwork/concept and packaging).

---

## 4. Relationship to sibling screens

**Shared "skin" / base.** licensing_tracking, prod_tracking, and sample_tracking are three instances of **one common AG Grid pattern**, not a shared base class — each has its own component but they were cloned from the same factory:
- All three column configs use the identical `addColumn(...) + commonDefaults` factory (`license.tracking.config.ts`, `sample.tracking.config.ts` whose header comment explicitly says *"Mirrors the addColumn / commonDefaults factory used by the other tracking grids (e.g. license.tracking.config.ts)"*, and `prod.tracking.detail.config.ts`).
- licensing_tracking and prod_tracking **share the same write endpoint**: both call `LicensingTrackingService.updateProdOrderHeader()` → `/lic/updateLic` is the licensing one; prod_tracking calls the same-named service method (`prod_tracking.component.ts:1542`) but **reads** via `trackingService.getProdOrderHeader()` (line 1183) against `ProdOrderHeader`. So they differ by **read endpoint + column config + underlying table** (`itemHeader` vs `ProdOrderHeader`), sharing the grid framework, view/layout system, cell-notes, and image/export plumbing.
- sample_tracking is the newest sibling (its own thin tables `sample`, `sample_event`, etc. per the config), reusing only the visual skin.

**RFQ relationship.** The licensing grid is **structurally the RFQ grid's sibling and partial clone**: the component imports RFQ sub-components (`rfqDatepickerComponent`, `rfqAutocompleteComponent`, `rfq/custom-tooltip`), its context menu calls `archivedRFQItem({url:'rfq'})`, `updateRfqItem`, `recalculate_Lic_Gen`, and references RFQ pricing fields (`fob_cost`, `whsecost`, `duty_rate`, `lic_fob_royalty`, `lic_*_sell_price/margin/price_sales`, `gen_*` equivalents) in `onDivisionCodeChange` and the duty/freight menus. RFQ is the **costing/pricing master grid**; licensing_tracking reuses that costing machinery but **its default visible columns are approval-tracking, not pricing**.

**Is licensing tracking costing or approval?** **Both by lineage, approval by current use.** The code carries a full RFQ-style costing apparatus (duty, freight recalculation, royalty/sell-price column show/hide per division, equation engine `recalculate_Lic_Gen`), but the **rendered default grid is purely approval/status-tracking** (status, licensor, artist, dates, BA#, sample#). The pricing columns exist in the schema and toggle logic but are **not in `license.tracking.config.ts`**, so in practice this is an **approval-tracking grid built on the RFQ costing chassis**.

---

## 5. Key field list (AG Grid `field` / `colId` for the licensing grid)

From `license.tracking.config.ts` (the `field` value is the display header; `colId` is the stable id — note this grid uses display names as `field` and remaps server-side via `mapFieldToColumn`):

| `field` (header) | `colId` | DB column (`mapFieldToColumn` / `remapLicItem`) | editable |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Picture` | `pic` | `itemAttachments[].attachment_link` (+`FullSizePicture`) | no (image editor) |
| `License Status` | `license_status` | derived: latest `licensingStatus.status` | yes (select) |
| `Item Number` | `item_num_id` | `item_num_id` | no |
| `Item Desc` | `item_desc` | `item_displ_descr_name` | yes |
| `Licensor Code` | `licensorCode` | `udf_merchgroup05_fk` → `merchGroup(05).mg_desc` | no |
| `For Customer` | `for_cust` | `udf_merchgroup15_fk` | yes |
| `Artist` | `artist` | `udf_merchgroup09_fk` → `merchGroup(09).mg_desc` | yes |
| `On Shelf Date` | `on_shelf_date` | `udf_date01` | no (date) |
| `Due Date` | `due_date` | `due_date` | no (date) |
| `Updated On` | `updated_date` | `lic_tracking_updated_date` | no (date) |
| `season` | `season` | `season_code_fk` | yes |
| `Brand Assurance Number` | `brand_assurance_number` | `udf_freeform_03` | no |
| `Sample No` | `sample_no` | `lic_sample_no` | yes |
| `Comment` | `comment` | latest `comments[].comment` else `lic_comment` | yes |

Hidden payload fields also returned per row (useful for matching another schema): `id` (=`item_id_pk`), `FullSizePicture`, `divcode` (`div_code_fk`), and `history[]` = array of `licensingStatus` rows (`{id, date, status, feedback, from, user, pkg}`).

**Server-side filterable field → column map** (`mapFieldToColumn`, `lic.model.js:222`): `item_num_id→item_num_id`, `item_desc→item_displ_descr_name`, `brand_assurance_number→udf_freeform_03`, `licensorCode→merchGroup05.udf_merchgroup05_fk_id`, `comment→lic_comment`, `season→season_code_fk`, `for_cust→udf_merchgroup15_fk`, `artist→merchGroup09.udf_merchgroup09_fk_id`, `sample_no→lic_sample_no`, `due_date→due_date`, `updated_date→lic_tracking_updated_date`, `on_shelf_date→udf_date01`.

---

## Key findings / caveats

1. **The row entity is a SKU/item (`itemHeader`)**, not a licensor submission. Submissions are child events in `licensingStatus`, surfaced only as the single latest status + a `history[]` array.
2. **Two backends, one schema:** grid reads/writes go to tracking-5002 `/lic/*`; the approval-feedback create/read goes to backend-5000 (`AddLicensingStatus`, `getLicenseFeedBacks`/`licenseList`). They share the same Postgres tables, so this split is purely a routing artifact.
3. **Large dormant schema:** `itemHeader` has ~40 `lic_*` workflow columns (concept/sample/prepo/order milestones) written by `mapLicItem` but absent from the visible grid — the live workflow has migrated to the `licensingStatus` event table. `licensingMilestone` appears entirely unused by this screen.
4. **Costing chassis, approval skin:** the component inherits RFQ pricing/duty/freight/royalty logic (division-based column show/hide, `recalculate_Lic_Gen`, duty dialog) but ships **no pricing columns** in the default config — those code paths are largely dead for this grid as configured.
5. **For schema-matching another system**, the 13 `colId`s in §5 plus the `mapFieldToColumn` table give the exact field-to-DB-column correspondence; the licensor/artist/customer fields are UDF-encoded merchGroup foreign keys (types `05`/`09`/`15`), and Brand Assurance # is `udf_freeform_03`.

Code-quality note worth flagging separately: `utility.js` lines 1463 and 1550 contain stray leading backslashes (`\ [colHeaders.dlvy_loc]` and `\sample_aprov_lead_day`) and `remapLicItem` reads `item.dataValues.deliveryLocations`/`company` which are not in the `getAllLicenses` include list — potential latent bugs, but outside the scope of this read-only report.

# System-Integration Investigation: DesignFlow PLM ↔ poppim/Directus

## Q1 — What `designflow-data-syncing` actually does

**It is a Coldlion → DesignFlow Postgres one-way sync service.** It is NOT connected to Directus/poppim in any way.

- **README** (`D:\repos\dflow\designflow-data-syncing\README.md:3`): *"Service responsible for synchronization flows between Designflow system and Coldlion."* Stack = Node/Express + PostgreSQL/Sequelize + Axios (`README.md:6-12`).
- **SOURCE** = **Coldlion ERP**, a REST API at `http://x5.coldlion.com/EhpApi/...`, authenticated with `COLDLION_API_KEY` (`X-API-Key` header). Hardcoded endpoints throughout `models\lib.model.js` (e.g. `:651` merchGroupHeaders, `:697` merchGroupDetails, `:778` prodtracking, `:822` vendors, `:855` customers, `:950` itemDetails, `:1042` prepackDetail).
- **DESTINATION** = the **shared DesignFlow PostgreSQL** (the PLM schema), via Sequelize. Connection in `models\db.js` (`dialect:'postgres'`, `host: DB_HOST`, `schema: process.env.SCHEMA`). So the topology is **External ERP API (Coldlion) → Postgres**, not Postgres→Postgres.
- **Entities synced** (`docs\system-interconnectivity.md:30-37`, confirmed in code): item headers/details (`itemHeader`, `itemDetail`), item images/attachments (downloaded then uploaded to **GCS** `creatiflow-attachment` bucket, `lib.model.js:497-517`), merch groups + merch-group headers, production orders (`ProdOrderHeader`/`ProdOrderDetail`), external vendors/customers, and prepack→item associations (`item_prod_order_detail_associations`). **No pricing-only or licensing-only sync job** beyond fields that ride along on the item records.
- **Trigger model**: HTTP routes gated by `X-API-Key` (`index.js:30-33`); reached through the BFF as `/api/data_sync/*`. **No cron/scheduler is present in the repo** — there is no node-cron, no Cloud Scheduler config, no `schedule:` block. A code comment says item checks run "every Saturday" (`lib.model.js:212`) but that cadence is **not implemented here**; jobs are invoked externally by hitting the routes.
- **Directus / data.designflow.app references: NONE.** Grep for `directus|poppim|designflow.app` returns only (a) doc text about the unrelated BFF custom domain `api.*.designflow.app`, and (b) the literal word in incident notes. No `data.designflow.app`, no Directus SDK, no `product`/`stage` collections.

**Key config quoted** — `.env.example`:
```
DB_HOST=localhost / DB_PORT=5432 / DB_NAME / SCHEMA=designflow_dev
DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY=...   COLDLION_API_KEY=...
```

## Q2 — Database topology: DesignFlow vs Directus

**Clearly SEPARATE databases on separate clouds.** Evidence:

- All four DesignFlow services (`designflow-backend`, `-item-master`, `-tracking`, `-data-syncing`) use an **identical** `config\db.config.js` that reads `DB_HOST/DB_PORT/DB_USER/DB_PASSWORD/DB_NAME` from env, plus a `SCHEMA` var. The schema name is `designflow` (prod, `designflow-backend\.env.example:4`) / `designflow_dev` (sync example).
- **The actual DB host/name values are NOT in the code** — every `cloudbuild.yaml` pulls `DB_HOST`, `DB_NAME`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_USER`, `DB_PASSWORD` from **GCP Secret Manager** (`designflow-backend\cloudbuild.yaml:48-52`, same in `-data-syncing\cloudbuild.yaml:40-45`). So I cannot read the literal hostname from the repo — but I can determine the cloud unambiguously (below).
- DesignFlow runs on **Google Cloud Run** in GCP project **`lithe-breaker-323913`**, region **us-central1** (`designflow-bff\CLAUDE.md`: "GCP project: lithe-breaker-323913, region: us-central1"). Cloud Run service URLs share the host token `mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` (`designflow-bff\env\.env.production:9-12`). DB access is via a `--vpc-connector` (private VPC) — consistent with a **Cloud SQL Postgres** instance in that GCP project.
- The Directus/poppim Postgres is **not in GCP at all**. Per `poppim-web\docs\architecture.md:11-13` and `AGENTS.md`, Directus is at `https://data.designflow.app`, the backend is the `u2giants/directus` repo, and it is deployed on **Coolify** (service uuid `ysvdyj3t7d5tyh5ogrvlka4y`) with GHCR images. DesignFlow's *storage* is also DigitalOcean Spaces (`designflow-backend\.env.example:12-17`, `nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com`).
- **No DesignFlow config references `data.designflow.app`, a `directus` database, or a shared Cloud SQL instance.** Conversely, no poppim/Directus config references the GCP instance.

**Verdict:** DesignFlow Postgres (GCP Cloud SQL, project `lithe-breaker-323913`, schema `designflow`) and the Directus Postgres (Coolify-hosted, behind `data.designflow.app`) are **separate database instances on separate infrastructure.** They are not the same DB.

- **DesignFlow product identity:** `itemHeader.item_num_id` is `DataTypes.STRING(100), allowNull: true` (`designflow-item-master\models\db\itemHeader.js:727-730`). It's the join key used internally for prepack matching (`data-syncing\models\lib.model.js:998-1001`: `where: { item_num_id: normalizedItemNo }`).

- **poppim side:** `Product.code: string | null` (`poppim-web\src\lib\types.ts:36`) is fetched in `src\features\board\api.ts:21`. Its purpose/format is not constrained in code.
- **Existing integration between the two: NONE.** No DesignFlow code mentions Directus/poppim/`pm.designflow.app`/a `product` table. No poppim code mentions DesignFlow, `item_num_id`, Coldlion, or the GCP backend (grep for `item_num|designflow|pim|external` in poppim returns only doc/deploy text). poppim *does* have integration-ready fields — `ProductLink.linked_external_id` and `ChecklistItem.source_id`/`source_system` (`types.ts:51-52, 147-157`) — but these are currently wired to ClickUp (`clickup_*` fields on `Product`), **not** DesignFlow. | Compute | Cloud Run, project `lithe-breaker-323913`, region us-central1 (`*.mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app`) | Coolify service `ysvdyj3t7d5tyh5ogrvlka4y`, project `jdq36h5dq74o6ddhich9l796` |

| DB | Cloud SQL Postgres (host in Secret Manager, via VPC connector) | Postgres behind `data.designflow.app` (Directus-owned) |
| Object storage | **DigitalOcean Spaces** (`nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com`) *and* GCS bucket `creatiflow-attachment` | (Directus storage) |
| Frontend host | `designflow.app` / `api.designflow.app` (Cloud Run + BFF) | `pm.designflow.app` (Coolify) |

There is no shared compute, no shared DB instance, and no shared object storage between the PLM and the Directus deployment.

## Bottom line for your design
1. `designflow-data-syncing` is a Coldlion-ERP → GCP-Postgres importer; it is irrelevant to and unaware of the Directus side.
2. DesignFlow's Postgres (GCP Cloud SQL, schema `designflow`) and poppim's Postgres (Coolify, via Directus) are **physically separate databases on separate clouds** — any integration must cross a network/cloud boundary (e.g. API-to-API or a new sync service), not a shared DB.

content.text:
I now have everything I need: deep models of both systems plus the hard infra facts. Here's the critical finding that shapes the whole answer — **the two systems are on entirely separate clouds and databases with zero existing integration**, and they overlap on exactly one thing: tracking the same licensed products and their approval status, one richly (DesignFlow) and one coarsely (poppim).

Let me write up the full strategy as a shareable document, then walk you through it.

input.content:
# Unifying Licensing Tracking: DesignFlow PLM ↔ poppim PM System

**Author:** prepared for Albert · **Date:** 2026-06-13
**Companion docs:** `business-process.md`, `pm-system-design.md` (the employee-monitoring synthesis)

---

## 0. The one-paragraph answer

Two systems are tracking the same licensed products in two different places. **DesignFlow** tracks the *licensor-approval workflow* richly and correctly (every submission, status, Brand Assurance number, sample/PPS step), but buries it inside a power-user pricing grid that only Albert's world uses. **poppim** (the new PM system the company is standing up to replace ClickUp) is *supposed* to own that workflow for everyone — Jessica, Liz, Adam, Licensing — but today it only has a single "stage" column with no real licensing data behind it. The fix is **not** to pick one and kill the other. It is to **split the job by what each system is genuinely best at**: poppim becomes the single home for *workflow & status* (using DesignFlow's already-proven licensing model as the blueprint), DesignFlow stays the single home for *costing & sourcing*, and the one shared object — the product/SKU — is kept in sync by a small bridge. The licensing-tracking grid inside DesignFlow is the duplicated piece, and it gets retired once poppim shows the same thing.

---

## 1. What each system actually is

### 1.1 DesignFlow PLM — the costing & sourcing engine (Albert's domain)
- **Stack/infra:** Angular + AG Grid front end; Node/Express + **PostgreSQL** (schema `designflow`) on **Google Cloud Run / Cloud SQL** (GCP project `lithe-breaker-323913`). Product master is imported from the **Coldlion ERP** by `designflow-data-syncing`.
- **What it's truly built for:** RFQ pricing, duty/freight, factory quotes, the ERP item master. This is the company's real costing brain — and in the process docs the team literally refers to *"Albert / China / DesignFlow"* as the place pricing and factory sourcing happen.
- **The licensing-tracking screen** (`pages/licensing_tracking/`) is a **bolt-on** built on the RFQ pricing "chassis." It shows **one row per product/SKU** (`itemHeader`) and — this is the valuable part — behind each row sits a **real, structured approval history**:
  - `licensingStatus` — a per-product **event log** of every status change (date, status, who, package-vs-art track, free-text feedback, attachments, who it's assigned to / which group is tagged).
  - `LicenseFeedBacks` — the **catalog of valid workflow phases/statuses** (Ready to Submit → Concept Submitted → Concept Approved/Rejected → Request Pre-Pro Sample → Pre-Pro Submitted → Pre-Pro Approved…), with separate **Package** and **License/Art** tracks.
  - **Brand Assurance number**, **Sample number**, licensor, artist, for-customer, on-shelf date, due date.
- **What it does NOT cover:** the upstream brief → design → buyer-pick stages, mass-production execution (that's a sibling `prod_tracking` screen), and the entire Spruce line. It's the licensor-approval loop and nothing else.

> **Bottom line:** DesignFlow already contains a working, in-production prototype of *exactly* the licensing-approval data model the `pm-system-design.md` doc says still needs to be built (Part 11.2: "new `submission` collection," "`revision_note` collection," "sample/pricing trackers"). That is the single most important fact in this whole analysis.

### 1.2 poppim — the new company-wide PM system (the ClickUp replacement)
- **Stack/infra:** React/Vite front end talking to a **Directus** backend (separate **PostgreSQL**) at `data.designflow.app`, hosted on **Coolify + DigitalOcean** — a *different cloud* from DesignFlow. The Directus schema lives in a separate repo (`u2giants/directus`), not in poppim-web.
- **What it's built for:** the broad, role-friendly PM system the process docs describe — one place for projects, products, stages, dashboards for Jessica/Liz/Jen/Adam, "nothing creative gets lost," seasonal planning.
- **Current state:** One genuinely working module — a **Kanban board** over ~15,000 products (imported from ClickUp) + a Projects page (651) + a task-detail modal. The other screens (My Work, Schedule, Notes, People, Settings) are **visual mock-ups** with no real data yet.
- **Licensing model:** **Essentially none.** `licensor` is just a name. Approval status is implied only by the **name of the stage column** a card sits in ("Licensor Review," "Approved"). There is **no submission, no Brand Assurance, no PPS/sample, no factory, no costing** in its data model. "Spruce" doesn't even appear in the code yet; POP-vs-Spruce is a free-text field.

### 1.3 The relationship today
**There is none.** Different clouds, different databases, no shared key, no API between them. The only things they share are the `designflow.app` domain name and the Microsoft sign-in. The "two places" problem is real and total: a licensing status updated in DesignFlow is invisible to poppim, and vice-versa.

---

## 2. The overlap — precisely where the duplication is

| Concept | DesignFlow (today) | poppim (today) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product / SKU | `itemHeader` (rich, ERP-sourced, costing) | `product` (15K, ClickUp-sourced, PM) | **Same object, two copies** |
| Licensor | merch-group code → name | `licensor.name` | Duplicated (name only in poppim) |
| Approval status | structured event log (`licensingStatus`) | a stage column name | **Duplicated — DesignFlow is far richer** |
| Brand Assurance # | `udf_freeform_03` | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Sample / PPS status | sample no + status events | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Submission history | full per-product history | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Retailer / buyer | merch-group fields | `retailer.name` | Duplicated |
| Project / offer grouping | (weak) | `product.project` (real) | Only in poppim |
| Costing / pricing | the whole RFQ engine | — | **Only in DesignFlow, and should stay there** |
| Reusable-concept inventory, role dashboards, Spruce line | — | (planned) | Belongs in poppim |

**Read this table as a Venn diagram:** the only true overlap is **product identity + licensor + approval status**. Everything else is something *one* system should own and the other shouldn't. That's the seam to cut along.

---

## 3. The recommended design — "split by system of record"

The process docs already decided the strategic direction: **the Directus/poppim PM system is the future single home for workflow**, and **DesignFlow is the costing/sourcing engine.** The integration just makes that explicit and removes the overlap.

```
                         ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
                         │  poppim / Directus  =  WORKFLOW of record  │
                         │  • projects ⇄ products (parent/child)      │
                         │  • stages & lifecycle states               │
                         │  • licensor submissions & approvals  ◄──┐  │
                         │  • Brand Assurance, PPS/sample status   │  │  (model lifted
                         │  • revisions, reusable-concept library  │  │   FROM DesignFlow)
                         │  • role dashboards (Jessica/Liz/Adam…)  │  │
                         └─────────────────▲─────────────────────────┘
                                           │  product identity + costing status
                                           │  (small sync bridge, API↔API)
                         ┌─────────────────▼─────────────────────────┐
                         │  DesignFlow PLM  =  COSTING of record       │
                         │  • Coldlion/ERP product master              │
                         │  • RFQ pricing, duty/freight, royalty       │
                         │  • factory quotes & sourcing                │
                         │  • prod-order execution                     │
                         │  (pricing stays HERE, hidden from designers)│
                         └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Three principles do all the work:

1. **One object, one owner per concern.** The product/SKU is shared, but *workflow/status* is owned by poppim and *cost/sourcing* is owned by DesignFlow. Neither stores the other's truth.
2. **Don't rebuild — lift.** DesignFlow's `LicenseFeedBacks` + `licensingStatus` model is the blueprint for the `submission` / `revision_note` / `stage_history` collections the PM docs want. Copy the design, don't reinvent it.
3. **A thin bridge, not a merge.** Because the two databases are on different clouds, they can't share tables. They link through a small API-to-API sync keyed on the product code — and poppim already has the field for it (`product_link.linked_external_id`).

---

## 4. The seam in detail — what moves where

**poppim / Directus gains (new collections, modeled on DesignFlow):**
- `submission` — one row per licensor submission attempt (concept / packaging / PPS), with submission number, Brand Assurance #, submit date, response, response date, track (Package vs Art).
- `submission_event` / `stage_history` — the status timeline (this is DesignFlow's `licensingStatus` reborn as the doc's stage-history).
- `revision_note` — structured revisions (source: Liz / buyer / licensor / factory), replacing scattered Teams/email/markups.
- product-level fields: `licensor` (+ `property`/style-guide), `sample_status`, `brand_assurance_number`, lifecycle state (active/waiting/stuck/parked/reusable/canceled/abandoned/complete).

**DesignFlow keeps (and stops duplicating):**
- The RFQ costing engine, factory sourcing, prod-order tracking, ERP master — its real value.
- It **stops being an approval tracker.** The `licensing_tracking` grid is retired (or made read-only) once poppim shows the same workflow.

**The bridge (new, small service):**
- Maps `itemHeader.item_num_id` (+ company/division) ⇄ `product.code` / `product_link.linked_external_id`.
- Carries product identity + a *costing-status* signal ("pricing requested / returned," a cost rollup) into poppim **without exposing actual prices to designers** (matches doc principle: constraints visible, pricing protected).

---

## 5. Phased roadmap (lowest-risk first)

**Phase 0 — Stop the double-entry fast (days, not months).**
Establish the shared product key, then one-way push from DesignFlow → poppim: the latest `licensingStatus` status, Brand Assurance #, sample #, licensor → onto the matching poppim product (status drives its board stage). Result: the PM board reflects DesignFlow automatically; **nobody updates status in two places.** Cheapest possible win, no schema changes in either system beyond a link field.

**Phase 1 — Build the real licensing model in Directus.**
Add the `submission` / `stage_history` / `revision_note` collections (copied from DesignFlow's proven design) to the `u2giants/directus` repo. Backfill history from DesignFlow.

**Phase 2 — Build poppim's licensing UI, flip the source of truth.**
Licensing dashboard, Liz review queue, submission detail (the docs already spec these). Once users edit licensing *in poppim*, retire DesignFlow's `licensing_tracking` grid. DesignFlow keeps RFQ/costing/prod.

**Phase 3 — Costing bridge both ways.**
poppim shows "waiting on pricing / pricing returned" and a read-only cost rollup pulled from DesignFlow's API — giving Jessica/Jen/Adam the visibility the docs ask for, while pricing itself never leaves DesignFlow.

---

## 6. Decisions only you can make (these change the build)

1. **Strategic direction.** The docs point to poppim as the company-wide system of record and DesignFlow receding to costing/sourcing. Is that the intent — or is DesignFlow meant to remain the licensing tracker, with poppim just *reading* from it? This flips who owns the data.
2. **The shared key is unverified.** `item_num_id` (e.g. `MEZH1DYPN02`) *could* join to poppim's `product.code`, but the two product sets came from different sources (Coldlion ERP vs ClickUp). Someone has to confirm the same products carry the same code in both — likely keyed on `item_num_id` + company + division. **This is the first thing to test before anything else.**
3. **Appetite.** Full consolidation (Phases 0–3) vs. just Phase 0 (kill the double-entry, leave both systems otherwise as-is).
4. **Who builds/owns the cross-cloud bridge** (it spans GCP ↔ Coolify and needs the `u2giants/directus` repo, which isn't checked out locally).

---

## 7. What I'd do first (recommendation)

Start with **Phase 0**, but gate it on **Decision #2**: pull a sample of product codes from both systems and confirm they line up. If they do, a one-way status sync stops the duplication almost immediately and proves the bridge — with no risk to either production system — before committing to the larger consolidation. Everything after that follows the roadmap the process docs already imply.

# Unifying Licensing Tracking: DesignFlow PLM ↔ poppim PM System

**Author:** prepared for Albert · **Date:** 2026-06-13
**Companion docs:** `business-process.md`, `pm-system-design.md` (the employee-monitoring synthesis)

---

## 0. The one-paragraph answer

Two systems are tracking the same licensed products in two different places. **DesignFlow** tracks the *licensor-approval workflow* richly and correctly (every submission, status, Brand Assurance number, sample/PPS step), but buries it inside a power-user pricing grid that only Albert's world uses. **poppim** (the new PM system the company is standing up to replace ClickUp) is *supposed* to own that workflow for everyone — Jessica, Liz, Adam, Licensing — but today it only has a single "stage" column with no real licensing data behind it. The fix is **not** to pick one and kill the other. It is to **split the job by what each system is genuinely best at**: poppim becomes the single home for *workflow & status* (using DesignFlow's already-proven licensing model as the blueprint), DesignFlow stays the single home for *costing & sourcing*, and the one shared object — the product/SKU — is kept in sync by a small bridge. The licensing-tracking grid inside DesignFlow is the duplicated piece, and it gets retired once poppim shows the same thing.

---

## 1. What each system actually is

### 1.1 DesignFlow PLM — the costing & sourcing engine (Albert's domain)
- **Stack/infra:** Angular + AG Grid front end; Node/Express + **PostgreSQL** (schema `designflow`) on **Google Cloud Run / Cloud SQL** (GCP project `lithe-breaker-323913`). Product master is imported from the **Coldlion ERP** by `designflow-data-syncing`.
- **What it's truly built for:** RFQ pricing, duty/freight, factory quotes, the ERP item master. This is the company's real costing brain — and in the process docs the team literally refers to *"Albert / China / DesignFlow"* as the place pricing and factory sourcing happen.
- **The licensing-tracking screen** (`pages/licensing_tracking/`) is a **bolt-on** built on the RFQ pricing "chassis." It shows **one row per product/SKU** (`itemHeader`) and — this is the valuable part — behind each row sits a **real, structured approval history**:
  - `licensingStatus` — a per-product **event log** of every status change (date, status, who, package-vs-art track, free-text feedback, attachments, who it's assigned to / which group is tagged).
  - `LicenseFeedBacks` — the **catalog of valid workflow phases/statuses** (Ready to Submit → Concept Submitted → Concept Approved/Rejected → Request Pre-Pro Sample → Pre-Pro Submitted → Pre-Pro Approved…), with separate **Package** and **License/Art** tracks.
  - **Brand Assurance number**, **Sample number**, licensor, artist, for-customer, on-shelf date, due date.
- **What it does NOT cover:** the upstream brief → design → buyer-pick stages, mass-production execution (that's a sibling `prod_tracking` screen), and the entire Spruce line. It's the licensor-approval loop and nothing else.

> **Bottom line:** DesignFlow already contains a working, in-production prototype of *exactly* the licensing-approval data model the `pm-system-design.md` doc says still needs to be built (Part 11.2: "new `submission` collection," "`revision_note` collection," "sample/pricing trackers"). That is the single most important fact in this whole analysis.

### 1.2 poppim — the new company-wide PM system (the ClickUp replacement)
- **Stack/infra:** React/Vite front end talking to a **Directus** backend (separate **PostgreSQL**) at `data.designflow.app`, hosted on **Coolify + DigitalOcean** — a *different cloud* from DesignFlow. The Directus schema lives in a separate repo (`u2giants/directus`), not in poppim-web.
- **What it's built for:** the broad, role-friendly PM system the process docs describe — one place for projects, products, stages, dashboards for Jessica/Liz/Jen/Adam, "nothing creative gets lost," seasonal planning.
- **Current state:** One genuinely working module — a **Kanban board** over ~15,000 products (imported from ClickUp) + a Projects page (651) + a task-detail modal. The other screens (My Work, Schedule, Notes, People, Settings) are **visual mock-ups** with no real data yet.
- **Licensing model:** **Essentially none.** `licensor` is just a name. Approval status is implied only by the **name of the stage column** a card sits in ("Licensor Review," "Approved"). There is **no submission, no Brand Assurance, no PPS/sample, no factory, no costing** in its data model. "Spruce" doesn't even appear in the code yet; POP-vs-Spruce is a free-text field.

### 1.3 The relationship today
**There is none.** Different clouds, different databases, no shared key, no API between them. The only things they share are the `designflow.app` domain name and the Microsoft sign-in. The "two places" problem is real and total: a licensing status updated in DesignFlow is invisible to poppim, and vice-versa.

---

## 2. The overlap — precisely where the duplication is

| Concept | DesignFlow (today) | poppim (today) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product / SKU | `itemHeader` (rich, ERP-sourced, costing) | `product` (15K, ClickUp-sourced, PM) | **Same object, two copies** |
| Licensor | merch-group code → name | `licensor.name` | Duplicated (name only in poppim) |
| Approval status | structured event log (`licensingStatus`) | a stage column name | **Duplicated — DesignFlow is far richer** |
| Brand Assurance # | `udf_freeform_03` | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Sample / PPS status | sample no + status events | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Submission history | full per-product history | — | Only in DesignFlow |
| Retailer / buyer | merch-group fields | `retailer.name` | Duplicated |
| Project / offer grouping | (weak) | `product.project` (real) | Only in poppim |
| Costing / pricing | the whole RFQ engine | — | **Only in DesignFlow, and should stay there** |
| Reusable-concept inventory, role dashboards, Spruce line | — | (planned) | Belongs in poppim |

**Read this table as a Venn diagram:** the only true overlap is **product identity + licensor + approval status**. Everything else is something *one* system should own and the other shouldn't. That's the seam to cut along.

---

## 3. The recommended design — "split by system of record"

The process docs already decided the strategic direction: **the Directus/poppim PM system is the future single home for workflow**, and **DesignFlow is the costing/sourcing engine.** The integration just makes that explicit and removes the overlap.

```
                         ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
                         │  poppim / Directus  =  WORKFLOW of record  │
                         │  • projects ⇄ products (parent/child)      │
                         │  • stages & lifecycle states               │
                         │  • licensor submissions & approvals  ◄──┐  │
                         │  • Brand Assurance, PPS/sample status   │  │  (model lifted
                         │  • revisions, reusable-concept library  │  │   FROM DesignFlow)
                         │  • role dashboards (Jessica/Liz/Adam…)  │  │
                         └─────────────────▲─────────────────────────┘
                                           │  product identity + costing status
                                           │  (small sync bridge, API↔API)
                         ┌─────────────────▼─────────────────────────┐
                         │  DesignFlow PLM  =  COSTING of record       │
                         │  • Coldlion/ERP product master              │
                         │  • RFQ pricing, duty/freight, royalty       │
                         │  • factory quotes & sourcing                │
                         │  • prod-order execution                     │
                         │  (pricing stays HERE, hidden from designers)│
                         └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Three principles do all the work:

1. **One object, one owner per concern.** The product/SKU is shared, but *workflow/status* is owned by poppim and *cost/sourcing* is owned by DesignFlow. Neither stores the other's truth.
2. **Don't rebuild — lift.** DesignFlow's `LicenseFeedBacks` + `licensingStatus` model is the blueprint for the `submission` / `revision_note` / `stage_history` collections the PM docs want. Copy the design, don't reinvent it.
3. **A thin bridge, not a merge.** Because the two databases are on different clouds, they can't share tables. They link through a small API-to-API sync keyed on the product code — and poppim already has the field for it (`product_link.linked_external_id`).

---

## 4. The seam in detail — what moves where

**poppim / Directus gains (new collections, modeled on DesignFlow):**
- `submission` — one row per licensor submission attempt (concept / packaging / PPS), with submission number, Brand Assurance #, submit date, response, response date, track (Package vs Art).
- `submission_event` / `stage_history` — the status timeline (this is DesignFlow's `licensingStatus` reborn as the doc's stage-history).
- `revision_note` — structured revisions (source: Liz / buyer / licensor / factory), replacing scattered Teams/email/markups.
- product-level fields: `licensor` (+ `property`/style-guide), `sample_status`, `brand_assurance_number`, lifecycle state (active/waiting/stuck/parked/reusable/canceled/abandoned/complete).

**DesignFlow keeps (and stops duplicating):**
- The RFQ costing engine, factory sourcing, prod-order tracking, ERP master — its real value.
- It **stops being an approval tracker.** The `licensing_tracking` grid is retired (or made read-only) once poppim shows the same workflow.

**The bridge (new, small service):**
- Maps `itemHeader.item_num_id` (+ company/division) ⇄ `product.code` / `product_link.linked_external_id`.
- Carries product identity + a *costing-status* signal ("pricing requested / returned," a cost rollup) into poppim **without exposing actual prices to designers** (matches doc principle: constraints visible, pricing protected).

---

## 5. Phased roadmap (lowest-risk first)

**Phase 0 — Stop the double-entry fast (days, not months).**
Establish the shared product key, then one-way push from DesignFlow → poppim: the latest `licensingStatus` status, Brand Assurance #, sample #, licensor → onto the matching poppim product (status drives its board stage). Result: the PM board reflects DesignFlow automatically; **nobody updates status in two places.** Cheapest possible win, no schema changes in either system beyond a link field.

**Phase 1 — Build the real licensing model in Directus.**
Add the `submission` / `stage_history` / `revision_note` collections (copied from DesignFlow's proven design) to the `u2giants/directus` repo. Backfill history from DesignFlow.

**Phase 2 — Build poppim's licensing UI, flip the source of truth.**
Licensing dashboard, Liz review queue, submission detail (the docs already spec these). Once users edit licensing *in poppim*, retire DesignFlow's `licensing_tracking` grid. DesignFlow keeps RFQ/costing/prod.

**Phase 3 — Costing bridge both ways.**
poppim shows "waiting on pricing / pricing returned" and a read-only cost rollup pulled from DesignFlow's API — giving Jessica/Jen/Adam the visibility the docs ask for, while pricing itself never leaves DesignFlow.

---

## 6. Decisions only you can make (these change the build)

1. **Strategic direction.** The docs point to poppim as the company-wide system of record and DesignFlow receding to costing/sourcing. Is that the intent — or is DesignFlow meant to remain the licensing tracker, with poppim just *reading* from it? This flips who owns the data.
2. **The shared key is unverified.** `item_num_id` (e.g. `MEZH1DYPN02`) *could* join to poppim's `product.code`, but the two product sets came from different sources (Coldlion ERP vs ClickUp). Someone has to confirm the same products carry the same code in both — likely keyed on `item_num_id` + company + division. **This is the first thing to test before anything else.**
3. **Appetite.** Full consolidation (Phases 0–3) vs. just Phase 0 (kill the double-entry, leave both systems otherwise as-is).
4. **Who builds/owns the cross-cloud bridge** (it spans GCP ↔ Coolify and needs the `u2giants/directus` repo, which isn't checked out locally).

---

## 7. What I'd do first (recommendation)

Start with **Phase 0**, but gate it on **Decision #2**: pull a sample of product codes from both systems and confirm they line up. If they do, a one-way status sync stops the duplication almost immediately and proves the bridge — with no risk to either production system — before committing to the larger consolidation. Everything after that follows the roadmap the process docs already imply.

# Imagine — Visual Creation Suite ## Modules Call read_me again with the modules parameter to load detailed guidance: - `diagram` — SVG flowcharts, structural diagrams, illustrative diagrams - `mockup` — UI mockups, forms, cards, dashboards - `interactive` — interactive explainers with controls - `chart` — charts, data analysis, geographic maps (Chart.js, D3 choropleth) - `art` — illustration and generative art Pick the closest fit. The module includes all relevant design guidance. **Complexity budget — hard limits:** - Box subtitles: ≤5 words. Detail goes in click-through (`sendPrompt`) or the prose below — not the box. - Colors: ≤2 ramps per diagram. If colors encode meaning (states, tiers), add a 1-line legend. Otherwise use one neutral ramp. - Horizontal tier: ≤4 boxes at full width (~140px each). 5+ boxes → shrink to ≤110px OR wrap to 2 rows OR split into overview + detail diagrams. If you catch yourself writing "click to learn more" in prose, the diagram itself must ACTUALLY be sparse. Don't promise brevity then front-load everything. **Accessibility:** For HTML widgets, begin with a visually-hidden `

` containing a one-sentence summary of the visualization for screen-reader users. (SVG widgets use `role="img"` with `` and `<desc>` instead — see SVG setup.) You create rich visual content — SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets — that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat. ## Core Design System These rules apply to ALL use cases. ### Philosophy - **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins. - **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces. - **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text. - **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** — All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks "explain X", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget. ### Streaming Output streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early. - **HTML**: `<style>` (short) → content HTML → `<script>` last. - **SVG**: `<defs>` (markers) → visual elements immediately. - Prefer inline `style="..."` over `<style>` blocks — inputs/controls must look correct mid-stream. - Keep `<style>` under ~15 lines. Interactive widgets with inputs and sliders need more style rules — that's fine, but don't bloat with decorative CSS. - Gradients, shadows, and blur flash during streaming DOM diffs. Use solid flat fills instead. ### Rules - No `<!-- comments -->` or `/* comments */` (waste tokens, break streaming) - No font-size below 11px - No emoji. Icons = Tabler **outline** webfont (5800+, already loaded): `<i class="ti ti-home"></i>`. Outline only — never use `-filled` suffixes (`ti-heart-filled` etc. are not loaded and will render blank). Inherits color + font-size from parent. Decorative icons get `aria-hidden="true"`; icon-only buttons get `aria-label`. Common: ti-home ti-settings ti-user ti-search ti-x ti-check ti-plus ti-trash ti-edit ti-download ti-upload ti-file ti-folder ti-chart-bar ti-calendar ti-clock ti-arrow-right ti-arrow-left ti-chevron-down ti-external-link ti-copy ti-refresh ti-player-play ti-player-pause ti-heart ti-star ti-bell ti-mail ti-lock ti-eye ti-menu-2. Don't hand-draw icon SVG paths. - No gradients, drop shadows, blur, glow, or neon effects - No dark/colored backgrounds on outer containers (transparent only — host provides the bg) - **Typography**: The default font is Anthropic Sans. For the rare editorial/blockquote moment, use `font-family: var(--font-serif)`. - **Headings**: h1 = 22px, h2 = 18px, h3 = 16px — all `font-weight: 500`. Heading color is pre-set to `var(--color-text-primary)` — don't override it. Body text = 16px, weight 400, `line-height: 1.7`. **Two weights only: 400 regular, 500 bold.** Never use 600 or 700 — they look heavy against the host UI. - **Sentence case** always. Never Title Case, never ALL CAPS. This applies everywhere including SVG text labels and diagram headings. - **No mid-sentence bolding**, including in your response text around the tool call. Entity names, class names, function names go in `code style` not **bold**. Bold is for headings and labels only. - The widget container is `display: block; width: 100%`. Your HTML fills it naturally — no wrapper div needed. Just start with your content directly. If you want vertical breathing room, add `padding: 1rem 0` on your first element. - Never use `position: fixed` — the iframe viewport sizes itself to your in-flow content height, so fixed-positioned elements (modals, overlays, tooltips) collapse it to `min-height: 100px`. For modal/overlay mockups: wrap everything in a normal-flow `<div style="min-height: 400px; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.45); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;">` and put the modal inside — it's a faux viewport that actually contributes layout height. - No DOCTYPE, `<html>`, `<head>`, or `<body>` — just content fragments. - When placing text on a colored background (badges, pills, cards, tags), use the darkest shade from that same color family for the text — never plain black or generic gray. - **Corners**: use `border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)` (or `-lg` for cards) in HTML. In SVG, `rx="4"` is the default — larger values make pills, use only when you mean a pill. - **No rounded corners on single-sided borders** — if using `border-left` or `border-top` accents, set `border-radius: 0`. Rounded corners only work with full borders on all sides. - **No titles or prose inside the tool output** — see Philosophy above. - **Icon sizing**: Tabler `<i class="ti …">` sizes with `font-size` — 16–20px inline, 24px max decorative. For one-off inline SVG icons, set `width`/`height` explicitly (same limits). - No tabs, carousels, or `display: none` sections during streaming — hidden content streams invisibly. Show all content stacked vertically. (Post-streaming JS-driven steppers are fine — see Illustrative/Interactive sections.) - No nested scrolling — auto-fit height. - Scripts execute after streaming — load libraries via `<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/...">` (UMD globals), then use the global in a plain `<script>` that follows. - **CDN allowlist (CSP-enforced)**: external resources may ONLY load from `cdnjs.cloudflare.com`, `esm.sh`, `cdn.jsdelivr.net`, `unpkg.com`, `fonts.googleapis.com`, `fonts.gstatic.com`. All other origins are blocked by the sandbox — the request silently fails. ### CSS Variables **Backgrounds**: `--color-background-primary` (white), `-secondary` (surfaces), `-tertiary` (page bg), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning` **Text**: `--color-text-primary` (black), `-secondary` (muted), `-tertiary` (hints), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning` **Borders**: `--color-border-tertiary` (0.15α, default), `-secondary` (0.3α, hover), `-primary` (0.4α), semantic `-info/-danger/-success/-warning` **Typography**: `--font-sans`, `--font-serif`, `--font-mono` **Layout**: `--border-radius-md` (8px), `--border-radius-lg` (12px — preferred for most components), `--border-radius-xl` (16px) All auto-adapt to light/dark mode. For custom colors in HTML, use CSS variables. **Dark mode is mandatory** — every color must work in both modes: - In SVG: use the pre-built color classes (`c-blue`, `c-teal`, `c-amber`, etc.) for colored nodes — they handle light/dark mode automatically. Never write `<style>` blocks for colors. - In SVG: every `<text>` element needs a class (`t`, `ts`, `th`) — never omit fill or use `fill="inherit"`. Inside a `c-{color}` parent, text classes auto-adjust to the ramp. - In HTML: always use CSS variables (--color-text-primary, --color-text-secondary) for text. Never hardcode colors like color: #333 — invisible in dark mode. - Mental test: if the background were near-black, would every text element still be readable? ### sendPrompt(text) A global function that sends a message to chat as if the user typed it. Use it when the user's next step benefits from Claude thinking. Handle filtering, sorting, toggling, and calculations in JS instead. ### Links `<a href="https://...">` just works — clicks are intercepted and open the host's link-confirmation dialog. Or call `openLink(url)` directly. ## When nothing fits Pick the closest use case below and adapt. Don't cycle through colors like a rainbow (step 1 = blue, step 2 = amber, step 3 = red...). Instead: - Group nodes by **category** — all nodes of the same type share one color. E.g. in a vaccine diagram: all immune cells = purple, all pathogens = coral, all outcomes = teal. - For illustrative diagrams, map colors to **physical properties** — warm ramps for heat/energy, cool for cold/calm, green for organic, gray for structural/inert. - Use **gray for neutral/structural** nodes (start, end, generic steps). - Use **2-3 colors per diagram**, not 6+. More colors = more visual noise. A diagram with gray + purple + teal is cleaner than one using every ramp. - **Prefer purple, teal, coral, pink** for general diagram categories. Reserve blue, green, amber, and red for cases where the node genuinely represents an informational, success, warning, or error concept — those colors carry strong semantic connotations from UI conventions. (Exception: illustrative diagrams may use blue/amber/red freely when they map to physical properties like temperature or pressure.) **Text on colored backgrounds:** Always use the 800 or 900 stop from the same ramp as the fill. Never use black, gray, or --color-text-primary on colored fills. **When a box has both a title and a subtitle, they must be two different stops** — title darker (800 in light mode, 100 in dark), subtitle lighter (600 in light, 200 in dark). Same stop for both reads flat; the weight difference alone isn't enough. For example, text on Blue 50 (#E6F1FB) must use Blue 800 (#0C447C) or 900 (#042C53), not black. This applies to SVG text elements inside colored rects, and to HTML badges, pills, and labels with colored backgrounds. **Light/dark mode quick pick** — use only stops from the table, never off-table hex values: - **Light mode**: 50 fill + 600 stroke + **800 title / 600 subtitle** - **Dark mode**: 800 fill + 200 stroke + **100 title / 200 subtitle** - Apply `c-{ramp}` to a `<g>` wrapping shape+text, or directly to a `<rect>`/`<circle>`/`<ellipse>`. Never to `<path>` — paths don't get ramp fill. For colored connector strokes use inline `stroke="#..."` (any mid-ramp hex works in both modes). Dark mode is automatic for ramp classes. Available: c-gray, c-blue, c-red, c-amber, c-green, c-teal, c-purple, c-coral, c-pink. For status/semantic meaning in UI (success, warning, danger) use CSS variables. For categorical coloring in both diagrams and UI, use these ramps. ## SVG setup **ViewBox safety checklist** — before finalizing any SVG, verify: 1. Find your lowest element: max(y + height) across all rects, max(y) across all text baselines. 2. Set viewBox height = that value + 40px buffer. 3. Find your rightmost element: max(x + width) across all rects. All content must stay within x=0 to x=680. 4. For text with text-anchor="end", the text extends LEFT from x. If x=118 and text is 200px wide, it starts at x=-82 — outside the viewBox. Increase x or use text-anchor="start". 5. Never use negative x or y coordinates. The viewBox starts at 0,0. 6. **No unintentional overlaps.** For every pair of elements that aren't meant to layer (label-on-label, label-on-arrow, box-on-box, callout-on-shape), check their bounding boxes do not intersect. The only allowed overlaps are deliberate: a label centered inside its own box, an arrowhead touching the box it points to, a highlight rect behind the thing it highlights. If two unrelated elements would collide, move one — shorten the label, shift the y, add a row. A diagram with crossed labels reads as broken regardless of how good the content is. 7. Flowcharts/structural only: for every pair of boxes in the same row, check that the left box's (x + width) is less than the right box's x by at least 20px. to `<path>` — paths don't get ramp fill. For colored connector strokes use inline `stroke="#..."` (any mid-ramp hex works in both modes). Dark mode is automatic for ramp classes. Available: c-gray, c-blue, c-red, c-amber, c-green, c-teal, c-purple, c-coral, c-pink.

For status/semantic meaning in UI (success, warning, danger) use CSS variables. For categorical coloring in both diagrams and UI, use these ramps.


## SVG setup

**ViewBox safety checklist** — before finalizing any SVG, verify:
1. Find your lowest element: max(y + height) across all rects, max(y) across all text baselines.
2. Set viewBox height = that value + 40px buffer.
3. Find your rightmost element: max(x + width) across all rects. All content must stay within x=0 to x=680.
4. For text with text-anchor="end", the text extends LEFT from x. If x=118 and text is 200px wide, it starts at x=-82 — outside the viewBox. Increase x or use text-anchor="start".
5. Never use negative x or y coordinates. The viewBox starts at 0,0.
6. **No unintentional overlaps.** For every pair of elements that aren't meant to layer (label-on-label, label-on-arrow, box-on-box, callout-on-shape), check their bounding boxes do not intersect. The only allowed overlaps are deliberate: a label centered inside its own box, an arrowhead touching the box it points to, a highlight rect behind the thing it highlights. If two unrelated elements would collide, move one — shorten the label, shift the y, add a row. A diagram with crossed labels reads as broken regardless of how good the content is.
7. Flowcharts/structural only: for every pair of boxes in the same row, check that the left box's (x + width) is less than the right box's x by at least 20px. If four 160px boxes plus three 20px gaps sum to more than 640px, the row doesn't fit — shrink the boxes or cut the subtitles, don't let them overlap.

**SVG setup**: `<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 680 H" role="img"><title>…</title><desc>…</desc>…` — 680px wide, flexible height. The root `<svg>` MUST carry `role="img"` with `<title>` and `<desc>` as its first children so screen readers can

lastPrompt: @"Z:\Documentation\company process - Oracle\pm-system-design.md" @"Z:\Documentation\company process - Oracle\business-process.md" i have a big job for you now. Understand in great detail the licensing…

content.text:
## The short version

I dug deep into both systems and the infrastructure behind them. Here's what's actually going on:

**You have two systems tracking the same licensed products, and they don't talk to each other at all** — literally different clouds, different databases, no shared key. That's the inefficiency, and it's total.

But here's the key insight that decides the whole approach: **each system is doing half of the other's job.**

- **DesignFlow** (your costing world) grew a licensing-tracking grid that quietly contains a *genuinely good, production-grade* model of the licensor-approval workflow — every submission, status, Brand Assurance number, sample/PPS step, even separate Package vs. Art tracks. It's buried inside a pricing grid only your team uses.
- **poppim** (the new company-wide PM system from your two docs) is *supposed* to own that workflow for Jessica, Liz, Adam, and Licensing — but today it only has a stage-name column with **no real licensing data behind it**. Its licensing model is essentially empty.

So they overlap on exactly one thing: **product identity + licensor + approval status.** Everything else is something only one of them should own.

**My recommendation — don't merge them, split them by what each is best at** (the diagram above):
- **poppim becomes the single home for workflow/status** — and instead of building the licensing model from scratch (which your `pm-system-design.md` says still needs doing), **copy DesignFlow's proven model into it.** DesignFlow already built the thing the docs are asking for.
- **DesignFlow stays the single home for costing/sourcing** — its real value, and where pricing stays protected from designers (matches your doc's principle).
- A **thin bridge** links them on the product code, and poppim already has the field for it.
- DesignFlow's licensing grid gets retired once poppim shows the same thing.

The full write-up — current state, exact field-level overlap, the target Directus collections, a 4-phase roadmap, and risks — is in [licensing-integration-strategy.md](../licensing-integration-strategy.md).

**Two things you should know before anything gets built:**
1. The shared product key is **unverified**. DesignFlow's products came from your Coldlion ERP; poppim's 15,000 came from ClickUp. Someone has to confirm the *same product carries the same code in both* before a bridge can work. This is the very first thing to test — and it's cheap.
2. The cheapest win (Phase 0) is a one-way "push status from DesignFlow into poppim" so nobody updates status twice — doable in days, no risk to either system, and it proves the bridge before you commit to the bigger consolidation.

One genuine fork in the road is yours to call, because it changes who owns the data:

questions.question: Strategic direction: which system should ultimately own the licensing-approval workflow?

options.label: poppim owns it (docs' direction)
options.description: Move the workflow into the PM system (poppim/Directus), reusing DesignFlow's proven model. DesignFlow recedes to costing/sourcing. Matches what your two process docs lay out. Biggest payoff, biggest effort.

options.label: DesignFlow stays owner, poppim reads it
options.description: Keep the rich approval tracking where it already works (DesignFlow), and just feed a read-only copy into poppim so the PM board reflects it. Less disruptive; poppim stays a viewer for licensing.

options.label: Just stop the double-entry for now
options.description: Do only Phase 0 — verify the shared key and one-way sync status into poppim. Leave the bigger question for later once that proves out.

options.label: Verify the shared key first
options.description: Pull sample product codes from both systems and check whether the same products line up — the gating fact before any bridge. (I may need DB/API access details.)

options.label: Draft the Phase 0 sync plan
options.description: Write a concrete technical plan for the one-way status bridge: endpoints, field mapping, where it runs across the two clouds.

options.label: Spec the Directus collections
options.description: Write the exact submission / stage_history / revision_note collection definitions for the u2giants/directus repo, modeled on DesignFlow's.

content.text:
Good — that's a clear, pragmatic direction: **keep the rich approval tracking in DesignFlow, feed poppim a read-only copy.** And the right first move is exactly what you picked: prove the products actually line up before designing any bridge.

To compare the two, I need to see real product codes from each side. Let me first find what's actually reachable from here — local DesignFlow DB credentials, and whether poppim's Directus answers read queries.

Got it — I'll do the analysis. I need two keys from you, and then I'll handle the rest:

**1. Local Postgres (for the DesignFlow side)**
- The password for your local PostgreSQL (I'll assume user `postgres` on `localhost:5432` unless you tell me otherwise).
- That's all — once I'm in, I'll find the right database/schema (`designflow_dev` / `designflow_sandbox` per your notes) and pull the `item_num_id` sample myself.

**2. Directus token (for the poppim side)**
- A static access token for `data.designflow.app`. Quickest way: in the Directus admin, open your user profile → **Token** field → generate one → Save → copy it. Read-only is fine — I only need to read `product.code`.
- You can revoke it right after I'm done.

Paste both here and I'll:
1. Pull ~100+ sample codes (and totals/null counts) from each system,
2. Check format compatibility, actual overlap, 1-to-1 uniqueness, and whether company/division is needed to disambiguate,
3. Give you a clear **match report** — the go/no-go fact that gates the whole bridge.

A quick heads-up so the result isn't a surprise: poppim's products were imported from **ClickUp**, while DesignFlow's come from your **Coldlion ERP** — so there's a real chance the codes *don't* natively match and we'll need a mapping (poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` field is built exactly for that). Either way, we'll know for certain rather than guessing.

One practical note: pasting a token in chat is fine for this, but since it's a credential, treat it as disposable — generate it for this task and revoke it when we're done.

content.text:
Perfect — both of those have clean workarounds. You don't actually need the token. Here's a turnkey packet for each side; the output of both is just a CSV you hand back to me.

---

## 1) DesignFlow side — give these to the developer

Ask them to run this against the DesignFlow PLM Postgres and send back the results (CSV is ideal). It's **read-only** — no writes, no changes.

**First, confirm the schema name** (it's almost certainly `designflow`, but this proves it):

```sql
SELECT table_schema
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_name = 'itemHeader';
```

Then run these three (replace `designflow` below if the query above returns something else):

```sql
-- A) Totals, how many codes are blank, and whether the code is unique on its own
SELECT
  count(*)                                                   AS total_rows,
  count(item_num_id)                                         AS non_null_codes,
  count(DISTINCT item_num_id)                                AS distinct_codes,
  count(DISTINCT (item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)) AS distinct_code_company_div
FROM designflow."itemHeader";

-- B) Breakdown by division (1/2/8 = licensed, 9 = generic) so we know the licensed slice
SELECT div_code_fk, count(*) AS rows
FROM designflow."itemHeader"
GROUP BY div_code_fk
ORDER BY rows DESC;

-- C) The actual code list to match against poppim — one row per product (send as CSV)
SELECT item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk, item_displ_descr_name
FROM designflow."itemHeader"
WHERE item_num_id IS NOT NULL AND item_num_id <> ''
ORDER BY item_id_pk DESC;
```

Query **C** is the important one — even 50,000 rows is a tiny CSV (it's basically one column of codes). Ask them to export C as CSV, plus paste the small results of A and B.

---

## 2) poppim side — you can do this yourself, no token needed

Directus hides the token field in a lot of setups, but you don't need it — **just export the collection to CSV from the admin UI:**

1. In Directus (`data.designflow.app`), click **Content** in the far-left icon bar.
2. Open the **product** collection (the list of ~15,000 products).
3. Top-right, click the **export icon** (a box with a down-arrow) — or the **"⋯"** menu → **Export Items**.
4. In the dialog: **Format = CSV**, **Limit = ` -1 `** (or "All" / a big number like 20000), and pick **Fields = `code`, `name`** (add `business_unit` and `licensor` if offered — helps me).
5. Click **Export / Download**. You'll get a `.csv` file.

If the export button isn't there either, tell me and I'll give you a one-line URL to paste into your browser while you're logged in that downloads the same CSV.

---

## Then

Drop both CSVs here (you can attach them, or just paste a few hundred rows from each). I'll run the overlap analysis — format match, how many codes appear in both, whether it's clean 1-to-1, and whether company/division is needed — and hand you the **go/no-go match report** that decides whether the bridge keys directly on the code or needs a mapping table.

content.text:
That means Directus ran the export as a background job and dropped the CSV into its **Files** module. To grab it:

1. In the far-left icon bar, click **File Library** (the icon that looks like a folder/photo stack — it's usually a few icons below **Content**).
2. The newest file at the top will be your export — named something like `product-2026-06-14….csv`.
3. Click it to open the file detail, then hit the **Download** button (a down-arrow icon, top-right).

That downloads the CSV to your computer — then drop it here.

A couple of quick notes:
- If you don't see it immediately, give it a few seconds and refresh — large exports (15K rows) take a moment to finish writing.
- If the File Library is cluttered, sort by date or just look for the most recent `.csv`.
