content.text:
Got it — and this is a genuinely important result. Let me give you the **match report (poppim side complete, DesignFlow side pending)**.

## What the poppim export revealed

**The obvious join key — the `code` field — is a dead end.** All 16,534 codes are lowercase ClickUp slugs (`1wnryyx`, `868g51ymz`). They have nothing to do with DesignFlow.

**But the real DesignFlow item number is hiding inside the product `name`.** Almost every licensed product's name *starts with* its DesignFlow item number, then the description:

> **`MTP78DYNX02`** Disney Block MDF perpetual calendar…  |  **`AA036MVAV02`** Marvel Avengers canvas…

That's the same format as DesignFlow's `item_num_id` (`MEZH1DYPN02`). Measured across the whole file:

| | Count | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total poppim "products" | 16,534 | |
| **Have an embedded DesignFlow item number** | **9,660 (58%)** | **9,293 distinct** — mostly 11-char, exact DF format |
| No item number | 6,874 (42%) | see below — mostly *not real products* |
| Same item-no on >1 product | 367 | re-offers/duplicates — expected |
| Business unit | 15,094 POP / 1,440 Spruce | (Spruce *is* in the data) |

**The 42% without a code aren't a problem — they're mostly ClickUp junk that was never going to match anyway.** The names tell the story:
- `"Update Status & Licensor Comments in DesignFlow"` ← literally a reminder-task to go update the *other* system
- `"Packaging concept submitted 1/10. OPA#114121811"` ← a submission event captured as free text
- `"Concept approved w/rev 8.10.2023: …"`, `"Jon's Picks 101320"`, `"Ross - Functional/Shelves - SENT TO ADAM"` ← comments, routing notes, presentation cards
- …plus a handful of genuine early-stage concepts (`"Plumbus wall hook"`) that simply never got a DesignFlow item number yet.

Two things worth pausing on:
1. **Your data literally proves the double-entry problem.** There are rows in poppim that are nothing but notes saying *"update the status in DesignFlow,"* and free-text submission numbers (OPA#…) — the exact events DesignFlow already stores in a clean, structured way. That's the strongest possible evidence for your chosen direction: keep the structured truth in DesignFlow, feed poppim from it.
2. **The bridge will key on the item number parsed from `name`, not the `code` field** — and a good cleanup step later is to write that item number into a proper field (poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` exists for exactly this).

## What's left

I have poppim's 9,293 item numbers saved. The last piece is **the DesignFlow side** — the CSV from query **C** I gave you for the developer. Once they send back the `item_num_id` list, I'll intersect the two and give you the final number: *of those 9,293, how many actually exist in DesignFlow* — the true go/no-go overlap. I'll also confirm whether the item number alone is unique or needs company+division (that's what query **A** answers).

So: still waiting on the developer's CSV (queries A, B, C). The moment you have it, drop it here and I'll close out the match report.

# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

=== HANDOFF.md ===
# HANDOFF — Remaining open items

Open items that need continuation context are listed below.

---

## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (updated 2026-06-11)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

### Files changed — frontend

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.scss` | **NEW** — drop zone, result, error banner styles |
| `src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts` | Added `getRFQFactoryTemplate()` (GET → blob) and `importFactoryRFQ(formData)` (POST → multipart) |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Added `onFactoryBulkUpload()` — decodes JWT for vendor name, opens dialog, refreshes grid + snackbar on success |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html` | Added `@if (role === 'vendor')` Upload Offers button in toolbar |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts` | Added `FactoryBulkUploadDialogComponent` to `declarations` |

### Files changed — backend (see `designflow-backend/HANDOFF.md`)

`helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `helpers/multer.js`, `models/lib.model.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`.

### Current state

- All changes are on `sandbox-albert`, **unstaged** (no `git add`, no `git commit`)
- `develop` was merged into `sandbox-albert` again on 2026-06-11 across the child repos; local uncommitted frontend/backend work was preserved.
- 2026-06-11 UX hardening added bilingual disabled-state/error feedback around this workflow: `Import Offers` now explains missing offer group name and/or `.xlsx` file; download/upload/file-type errors are user-visible in English and Simplified Mandarin.
- Backend copy guard messaging was also clarified to name missing/inactive Product Type / Sub-Type / Sub-Sub-Type instead of only the old pre-May-2025 wording.
- Backend: `npm test -- --runInBand rfqCopyMerchGroupGuard` passed on 2026-06-11.
- Frontend focused tests passed on 2026-06-11 via `npm test -- --testPathPatterns=addNewProduct.component --runInBand`, `addNewProductGrid`, `duplicate_dialog`, `rfq.component`, and `rfq-status-validation`.
- There is no `rfq-button` spec; vendor decline / Keep Price snackbar changes were not covered by a dedicated test.
- **Per push policy: do not push until Albert says so**

### Next action

When Albert says "push":
```bash
# Frontend
cd "D:/repos/dflow/designflow-frontend"
git add src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/ \
        src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts \
        src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts \

790	### 2026-05-31 — RFQ detail-grid price sort shipped as a silent no-op (wrong colId)
791	
792	**What happened:** The new "default-sort the vendor-quotes detail grid by price, lowest first" feature was committed and pushed (`e8ba0551`), but on screen nothing changed — the `Name` column still owned the sort and prices were unordered.
793	
794	**Root cause:** The sort (`sort`/`sortIndex`/`comparator`) was set only on the column with colId `quote_price`. The detail grid loads its columns from the backend `GridLayout` table (`getGridHeader({ grid_id: 'rfq_detail' })`), and the live `rfq_detail` layout serves the visible price column under the **legacy colId `price`**, not `quote_price`. The sort landed on a non-existent column, so AG Grid applied no price sort and `Name`'s `colDef.sort` kept winning.
795	
796	**Recovery:** (`b241ce79`) Extracted `applyDefaultPriceSort()`, which targets whichever price colId the layout provides (`quote_price` or `price`, preferring the visible one), and made the comparator read `node.data.Price` directly so it is independent of colId/field/valueGetter. Verified with unit tests covering both colIds and the unpriced/declined bottom-sink rule.
797	
798	**Rule added:** The detail grid's columns are backend-driven (`GridLayout`), so do not assume their `colId`s match the in-repo `rfq.detail.grid.config.ts`. When wiring behavior onto a detail-grid column by colId, match against the colIds the running layout actually serves (currently `price` for the price column), and prefer reading row data over the cell value in comparators. See §11 → "Detail-grid default sort targets the live price colId, not a hardcoded one".
799	
800	---

input.content:
# Licensing Tracking ↔ poppim PM — system overlap & integration direction

**Status:** analysis complete; shared-key **verification IN PROGRESS** (see `HANDOFF.md`).
**Date:** 2026-06-13/14. A fuller strategy write-up exists as an out-of-repo working artifact
(`licensing-integration-strategy.md` in the workspace root); this file is the durable in-repo summary.

## Why this exists

The `licensing_tracking` module (this repo, `src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/`) and a separate
project-management system **poppim** (`github.com/u2giants/poppim-web`, a React + Directus app at
`pm.designflow.app`) **both track the same licensed products and their approval status** — i.e. the
same work is entered in two places. This documents what each system is, where they overlap, the chosen
direction, and the key-matching finding that gates any integration.

## The two systems

- **DesignFlow `licensing_tracking` = the structured, production licensor-approval tracker.**
  One grid row = a product/SKU (`itemHeader`). The approval history is the `licensingStatus` event log;
  the valid phase/status catalog is `LicenseFeedBacks`; Brand Assurance # is `udf_freeform_03`; licensor/
  artist/for-customer are merch-group UDF FKs (`udf_merchgroup05/09/15`). It models dual **Package vs Art**
  submission tracks and assignee / tagged-group routing. Backed by the GCP Cloud SQL `designflow` schema,
  fed from the **Coldlion ERP** via `designflow-data-syncing`. (Built on the RFQ costing chassis, but the
  default grid shows approval/status columns, not pricing.)

- **poppim = the company-wide ClickUp replacement** (Directus backend, separate Postgres). Today its
  licensing model is essentially a single `stage` *name* per product — **no submission / Brand Assurance /
  PPS / costing data model.** ~16.5K products were imported from ClickUp (POP Creations 15,094 / Spruce 1,440).

## Infra reality (matters for any bridge)

The two run on **entirely separate clouds and databases** (DesignFlow = GCP Cloud Run / Cloud SQL;
poppim = Coolify + DigitalOcean, behind Directus at `data.designflow.app`). There is **no existing
integration and no shared key.** Any bridge crosses a network boundary (**API ↔ API**), not a shared DB —
do not assume a SQL join across the two is possible.

## Chosen direction (Albert, 2026-06-13)

**DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim consumes a read-only copy.**
This is a split-by-concern, not a merge: DesignFlow keeps the structured approval (and costing) truth,
poppim is the PM viewing/working surface. The richer, in-use model already lives in DesignFlow, so it is
the source; poppim's coarse stage column becomes a downstream reflection.

## Shared-key finding (poppim side measured 2026-06-14)

- poppim **`product.code` is a dead end** — all values are lowercase ClickUp slugs (6–9 chars), unrelated
  to DesignFlow.
- The **DesignFlow `item_num_id` is embedded as the prefix of poppim `product.name`**, e.g.
  `"MTP78DYNX02 Disney Block MDF perpetual calendar …"`. The token format matches
  `itemHeader.item_num_id` (≈11-char uppercase alphanumeric, same shape as `MEZH1DYPN02`).
- **Coverage:** 9,660 / 16,534 rows (58%) carry a parseable item number; **9,293 distinct**; 367 item-nos
  appear on >1 product (re-offers/duplicates).
- The ~42% with no code are **mostly not products** — ClickUp comments / routing notes / presentation cards
  (e.g. *"Update Status & Licensor Comments in DesignFlow"*, *"Packaging concept submitted 1/10. OPA#114121811"*)
  plus a few genuine early-stage concepts never SKU'd in the PLM. They were never going to match.
- **Implication for the bridge:** key on the item number **parsed from `product.name`** (not `product.code`),
  filter out the non-product rows, and ideally backfill poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` with the
  DesignFlow item number as a one-time cleanup so future joins don't depend on name-parsing.

To reproduce the poppim side: export the Directus `product` collection to CSV and take the first
whitespace-delimited token of `name` where it matches `/^[A-Z0-9]{9,13}$/` with ≥2 digits and ≥3 letters.

## Open verification (blocks go/no-go)

Need DesignFlow's `item_num_id` list (developer-run read-only queries, in `HANDOFF.md`) to **intersect with
the 9,293 poppim item-nos** and confirm true overlap, plus whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs
`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`. See `HANDOFF.md` → "Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration".

### 2026-06-13 — Phantom `rfq.component.spec` failure after a develop merge bumped AG Grid to 35.3

**What happened:** After merging `develop` into `sandbox-albert` and pulling locally, `yarn test --testPathPatterns=rfq` failed to compile `rfq.component.spec.ts` with TS2551/TS2724 errors: `gridOptions.notesDataSource`, `GridApi.refreshNotes`, and the `NotesDataSource` type "do not exist". The cell-notes feature merged from `develop` looked broken.

**Root cause:** The develop merge bumped `package.json` to `ag-grid-*@^35.3.0` (the new cell-notes feature requires those 35.3-only APIs), but local `node_modules` was still `35.2.1`. The code referenced 35.3 APIs the *installed* types didn't have. Nothing was wrong with the code — the installed dependencies were stale relative to the freshly merged `package.json`/`yarn.lock`.

**Recovery:** `yarn install` (pulled `ag-grid-*@35.3.1`). All 17 rfq suites then passed (268 tests); full suite 48 suites / 562 tests green; `npx tsc -p tsconfig.app.json --noEmit` clean.

**Rule added:** ts-jest does not surface a dependency-version mismatch *as* a version problem — it reads as "property/type does not exist" in whatever spec happens to touch the new API. After any `develop` merge (or any pull) that changes `package.json`/`yarn.lock`, run `yarn install` **before** trusting test or type failures. Do not "fix" code to match stale installed types.

---

## 15. Pending work

### 2026-06-13 — Phantom `rfq.component.spec` failure after a develop merge bumped AG Grid to 35.3

**What happened:** After merging `develop` into `sandbox-albert` and pulling locally, `yarn test --testPathPatterns=rfq` failed to compile `rfq.component.spec.ts` with TS2551/TS2724 errors: `gridOptions.notesDataSource`, `GridApi.refreshNotes`, and the `NotesDataSource` type "do not exist". The cell-notes feature merged from `develop` looked broken.

**Root cause:** The develop merge bumped `package.json` to `ag-grid-*@^35.3.0` (the new cell-notes feature requires those 35.3-only APIs), but local `node_modules` was still `35.2.1`. The code referenced 35.3 APIs the *installed* types didn't have. Nothing was wrong with the code — the installed dependencies were stale relative to the freshly merged `package.json`/`yarn.lock`.

**Recovery:** `yarn install` (pulled `ag-grid-*@35.3.1`). All 17 rfq suites then passed (268 tests); full suite 48 suites / 562 tests green; `npx tsc -p tsconfig.app.json --noEmit` clean.

**Rule added:** ts-jest does not surface a dependency-version mismatch *as* a version problem — it reads as "property/type does not exist" in whatever spec happens to touch the new API. After any `develop` merge (or any pull) that changes `package.json`/`yarn.lock`, run `yarn install` **before** trusting test or type failures. Do not "fix" code to match stale installed types.

---

## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — shared-key verification (updated 2026-06-14)

**Status:** BLOCKED — waiting on a read-only DesignFlow DB export from the PLM developer.

**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md`. Albert's direction (2026-06-13):
DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (the Directus PM app at
`pm.designflow.app`) consumes a **read-only** copy. Before any bridge is built, confirm the same products
carry a matchable key in both systems.

**Done (this session):**
- Deep-dived both data models (summary in the docs file); confirmed the two run on **separate clouds/DBs
  with no existing integration and no shared key** — any bridge is API↔API, not a SQL join.
- Measured the poppim side from a Directus `product` CSV export: the join key is the DesignFlow
  `item_num_id` embedded as the **prefix of poppim `product.name`** (NOT `product.code`, which is a ClickUp
  slug). **9,293 distinct** item-nos across **58%** of 16,534 rows; 367 duplicates; the ~42% without a code
  are mostly non-product ClickUp noise.

**Next action:**
1. Have the developer run queries **A/B/C** below against the DesignFlow Postgres (read-only) and return
   query **C** as CSV.
2. Intersect DesignFlow `item_num_id` (C) with the 9,293 poppim item-nos → **true overlap %** (go/no-go).
3. Query **A** tells whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
4. Produce the match report; if overlap is high, design the read-only status bridge keyed on the item number.

**Queries for the developer** (replace `designflow` if the schema-discovery query returns another schema):

```sql
-- schema check
SELECT table_schema FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_name = 'itemHeader';

-- A) totals, blank codes, and whether the code is unique on its own
SELECT count(*) AS total_rows, count(item_num_id) AS non_null_codes,
       count(DISTINCT item_num_id) AS distinct_codes,
       count(DISTINCT (item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)) AS distinct_code_company_div
FROM designflow."itemHeader";

-- B) breakdown by division (1/2/8 = licensed, 9 = generic)
SELECT div_code_fk, count(*) AS rows FROM designflow."itemHeader" GROUP BY div_code_fk ORDER BY rows DESC;

-- C) the code list to match against poppim (export as CSV)
SELECT item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk, item_displ_descr_name
FROM designflow."itemHeader"
WHERE item_num_id IS NOT NULL AND item_num_id <> ''
ORDER BY item_id_pk DESC;
```

**Risks / watchouts:**
- `item_num_id` is `STRING(100)` and company/division-scoped — the same code may repeat across
  company/division, so the bridge key may need `(item_num_id + company + division)`, not `item_num_id` alone
  (confirm with query A).
- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.
- The bridge is **API↔API across GCP↔Coolify**; there is no shared DB. Do not assume a SQL join is possible.

---

Delete this file once all items above are resolved.

# HANDOFF — Remaining open items

Open items that need continuation context are listed below.

---

## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (updated 2026-06-11)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

### Files changed — frontend

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.scss` | **NEW** — drop zone, result, error banner styles |
| `src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts` | Added `getRFQFactoryTemplate()` (GET → blob) and `importFactoryRFQ(formData)` (POST → multipart) |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Added `onFactoryBulkUpload()` — decodes JWT for vendor name, opens dialog, refreshes grid + snackbar on success |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html` | Added `@if (role === 'vendor')` Upload Offers button in toolbar |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts` | Added `FactoryBulkUploadDialogComponent` to `declarations` |

### Files changed — backend (see `designflow-backend/HANDOFF.md`)

`helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `helpers/multer.js`, `models/lib.model.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`.

### Current state

- All changes are on `sandbox-albert`, **unstaged** (no `git add`, no `git commit`)
- `develop` was merged into `sandbox-albert` again on 2026-06-11 across the child repos; local uncommitted frontend/backend work was preserved.
- 2026-06-11 UX hardening added bilingual disabled-state/error feedback around this workflow: `Import Offers` now explains missing offer group name and/or `.xlsx` file; download/upload/file-type errors are user-visible in English and Simplified Mandarin.
- Backend copy guard messaging was also clarified to name missing/inactive Product Type / Sub-Type / Sub-Sub-Type instead of only the old pre-May-2025 wording.
- Backend: `npm test -- --runInBand rfqCopyMerchGroupGuard` passed on 2026-06-11.
- Frontend focused tests passed on 2026-06-11 via `npm test -- --testPathPatterns=addNewProduct.component --runInBand`, `addNewProductGrid`, `duplicate_dialog`, `rfq.component`, and `rfq-status-validation`.
- There is no `rfq-button` spec; vendor decline / Keep Price snackbar changes were not covered by a dedicated test.
- **Per push policy: do not push until Albert says so**

### Next action

When Albert says "push":
```bash
# Frontend

## 1. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

Nothing fires `createNotification()` on business events. The notification system exists
(routes, service, frontend bell/badge) but no triggers have been wired.

**Pattern to follow** (from the existing @mention handler):

```js
setImmediate(async () => {
  try {
    await NotificationService.createManyNotifications(targets, {
      type: 'task_assignment',  // or 'status_change', 'approval'
      event: 'assigned',
      title: 'Task assigned',
      message: `${authorName} assigned a task to you`,
    });
  } catch {}
});
```

**Where to wire it:**
- Task assignment: `services/task.service.js` or wherever `createTask` / `assignTask` lives
- RFQ status change: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `rfq_status`
- Licensing approval: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `licensing_status`

| `type` | Icon |
|---|---|
| `mention` | alternate_email |
| `task_assignment` | assignment_ind |
| `approval` | check_circle |
| anything else | notifications |

## 3. RAG step 1: keyword search over hts-rates.json (backend — designflow-backend)

Add a `/api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}` endpoint that does a simple keyword match
over description fields in `data/hts-rates.json`. Inject the top 5 matches into the AI's
initial message as "Potentially relevant HTS headings." Zero new infrastructure — uses the
already-bundled USITC JSON file.

**Why it matters:** The system prompt covers 9 chapters. For products outside those chapters,
the model falls back on training data. A simple keyword search would inject the exact USITC
subheadings the model should consider, improving accuracy on edge-case products.

**Implementation sketch:**
- Backend: `helpers/hts-rates.js` — add `searchByDescription(text, limit = 5)` that filters
  `hts-rates.json` entries whose `description` field contains any token from `text`.
- Route: `routes/admin.router.js` — `GET /api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}`
- Frontend: `ai-settings.service.ts` — add `searchHtsRates(desc: string)` method
- Inject in `initAiSession()` alongside CROSS results (non-fatal if empty or missing)

+## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — shared-key verification (updated 2026-06-14)
+**Status:** BLOCKED — waiting on a read-only DesignFlow DB export from the PLM developer.
+**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md`. Albert's direction (2026-06-13):
+DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (the Directus PM app at
+`pm.designflow.app`) consumes a **read-only** copy. Before any bridge is built, confirm the same products
+carry a matchable key in both systems.
+**Done (this session):**
+- Deep-dived both data models (summary in the docs file); confirmed the two run on **separate clouds/DBs
+  with no existing integration and no shared key** — any bridge is API↔API, not a SQL join.
+- Measured the poppim side from a Directus `product` CSV export: the join key is the DesignFlow
+  `item_num_id` embedded as the **prefix of poppim `product.name`** (NOT `product.code`, which is a ClickUp
+  slug). **9,293 distinct** item-nos across **58%** of 16,534 rows; 367 duplicates; the ~42% without a code
+  are mostly non-product ClickUp noise.
+**Next action:**
+1. Have the developer run queries **A/B/C** below against the DesignFlow Postgres (read-only) and return
+   query **C** as CSV.
+2. Intersect DesignFlow `item_num_id` (C) with the 9,293 poppim item-nos → **true overlap %** (go/no-go).
+3. Query **A** tells whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
+4. Produce the match report; if overlap is high, design the read-only status bridge keyed on the item number.
+**Queries for the developer** (replace `designflow` if the schema-discovery query returns another schema):
+```sql
+-- schema check
+SELECT table_schema FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_name = 'itemHeader';
+-- A) totals, blank codes, and whether the code is unique on its own
+SELECT count(*) AS total_rows, count(item_num_id) AS non_null_codes,
+       count(DISTINCT item_num_id) AS distinct_codes,
+       count(DISTINCT (item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)) AS distinct_code_company_div
+FROM designflow."itemHeader";
+-- B) breakdown by division (1/2/8 = licensed, 9 = generic)
+SELECT div_code_fk, count(*) AS rows FROM designflow."itemHeader" GROUP BY div_code_fk ORDER BY rows DESC;
+-- C) the code list to match against poppim (export as CSV)
+SELECT item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk, item_displ_descr_name
+FROM designflow."itemHeader"
+WHERE item_num_id IS NOT NULL AND item_num_id <> ''
+ORDER BY item_id_pk DESC;
+**Risks / watchouts:**
+- `item_num_id` is `STRING(100)` and company/division-scoped — the same code may repeat across
+  company/division, so the bridge key may need `(item_num_id + company + division)`, not `item_num_id` alone
+  (confirm with query A).
+- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
+  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.
+- The bridge is **API↔API across GCP↔Coolify**; there is no shared DB. Do not assume a SQL join is possible.
+Delete this file once all items above are resolved.

content.text:
**GO. The key works — 99.3% match.**

## Match report — final

Of the 9,293 distinct item numbers I pulled from poppim's product names, **9,224 exist in DesignFlow.** Only 69 don't — and most of *those* are visibly truncated codes (`FBX55DABB`, `MBX25SADV`, `HSZ10SAC3` — missing their trailing digits, mangled during the ClickUp import). The real match rate is effectively ~100% for any product whose code wasn't garbled.

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Do the same products carry a matchable key in both? | **Yes — 99.3%** (9,224 / 9,293) |
| What's the key? | DesignFlow `item_num_id` ⇄ the item-number **prefix of poppim's `product.name`** (the `code` field is useless) |
| Is the key unique on its own? | **No** — needs `item_num_id` + company + division (query A: 19,321 codes → 17,775 distinct; ~1,500 repeat). Bridge should key on the composite. |
| What kind of products match? | **Almost entirely licensed** — 9,214 licensed (div 1/2/8) vs only 10 generic. This is squarely a *licensed-product* bridge, exactly the licensing-tracking use case. |
| Coverage direction | DesignFlow is the **superset**: 8,551 of its items (48%) aren't in poppim at all — older/archived/never-in-ClickUp products. |

## What this means

- **The read-only bridge is viable.** You can reliably point any poppim licensed product at its DesignFlow record and pull the structured approval status across. No fuzzy matching, no manual mapping table needed.
- **It's a one-way licensed-product mirror** (DesignFlow → poppim), which is exactly the direction you chose.
- **Two practical notes for whoever builds it:**
  1. Key on `item_num_id` + company + division (item number alone has ~1,500 collisions), and de-dupe the 367 poppim products that share an item number.
  2. As a one-time cleanup, write the matched item number into poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` so future syncs join on a real field instead of parsing names.
- The 8,551 DesignFlow-only items are a **later** decision (do you want poppim to know about them too?), not a blocker for the mirror.

---

This resolves the verification that was marked "blocked" in the docs/PR. Two things I can do next — your call:

input.content:
# Phase-0 Bridge — DesignFlow licensing status → poppim (read-only mirror)

**Status:** design. Verification is a confirmed GO (99.3% key match — see
`licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md`). This spec describes the smallest bridge that stops the
double-entry: a one-way, read-only mirror of each licensed product's approval status from DesignFlow
(system of record) into poppim (viewer).

## 1. Objective & scope

- **One-way:** DesignFlow → poppim. poppim never writes back.
- **Read-only in poppim:** the mirrored fields are display-only; users stop hand-updating licensing
  status in poppim because the real status arrives automatically.
- **Licensed products only:** the key match is ~entirely licensed items (div 1/2/8); generic/Spruce is
  out of scope for Phase-0.
- **No board disruption (Phase-0 default):** write the status to a **read-only field + badge**, do NOT
  overwrite the user-draggable `stage` yet. Driving the board `stage` from DF is a Phase-2 decision (it
  competes with manual drags). A config flag can enable stage-write later once the mapping is validated.

## 2. The join key (confirmed)

- Key = DesignFlow `itemHeader.item_num_id` ⇄ the **item-number prefix of poppim `product.name`**
  (the first whitespace token, matching `/^[A-Z0-9]{9,13}$/` with ≥2 digits & ≥3 letters).
  poppim `product.code` is a ClickUp slug and is NOT used.
- Match rate: 9,224 / 9,293 distinct poppim item-nos found in DesignFlow (**99.3%**); the ~0.7% misses
  are truncated/garbled codes from the ClickUp import.
- **Uniqueness:** `item_num_id` alone is NOT unique (~1,500 DF collisions; 367 poppim products share an
  item-no). Match on the composite **`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`** and, where a poppim
  side still resolves to >1 DF row, take the most-recently-updated DF record.
- **Backfill (one-time):** parse the item number out of `product.name` and write it to poppim
  `product_link.linked_external_id` (a field that already exists for cross-system links). After backfill,
  the sync joins on that stored field instead of re-parsing names every run.

## 3. Data flow

```
DesignFlow (GCP) ──read──►  bridge worker  ──map+write──►  poppim/Directus (Coolify)
 latest licensingStatus      (scheduled)     PATCH product.df_* fields
 per itemHeader              join on          (service-account token)
 + BA# + sample_no           linked_external_id
```

Cross-cloud, **API ↔ API** (no shared DB). Three stages: read DF status, transform/map, write poppim.

## 4. Status → stage/field mapping  (PROVISIONAL — finalize from the two real enumerations)

DesignFlow status comes from the `LicenseFeedBacks` catalog / latest `licensingStatus.status`. poppim
stage names come from the Directus `stage` collection. Below is a provisional mapping using the statuses
seen in `licensing_tracking`; **confirm against the real catalogs before building** (see §8 inputs).

| DesignFlow status (latest `licensingStatus`) | Mirrored `df_license_status` | Provisional poppim stage (Phase-2 only) |
|---|---|---|
| Ready to Submit / internal review | Ready to Submit | Licensor Review |
| Concept Submitted / Packaging Concept Submitted | Concept Submitted | Licensor Review |
| Concept Approved | Concept Approved | Approved |
| Concept Not Approved / rejected / revision | Revision Requested | In Development |
| Request Pre-Pro Sample | Sample Requested | Sampling |
| Pre-Pro Submitted | PPS Submitted | Sampling |
| Pre-Pro Approved | Pre-Pro Approved | Production |
| (production approved / closed) | Complete | Shipped/Complete |

Notes: DesignFlow tracks **dual Package vs Art tracks** — Phase-0 mirrors the single latest overall
status; preserving both tracks is a later enhancement.

## 5. poppim schema additions (read-only fields on `product`)

Add to the `u2giants/directus` schema (NOT this repo). All display-only:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `df_license_status` | string | mirrored latest DesignFlow status (drives a badge) |
| `df_brand_assurance` | string | `itemHeader.udf_freeform_03` |
| `df_sample_no` | string | `itemHeader.lic_sample_no` |
| `df_status_synced_at` | timestamp | last successful sync for this product |
| `product_link.linked_external_id` | (existing) | the DesignFlow `item_num_id` (backfilled) |

## 6. Sync mechanism & hosting

- **Cadence:** scheduled (e.g. every 15–30 min) + an on-demand trigger. Licensing status is not
  second-by-second.
- **Idempotent:** compute the desired field set per matched product, PATCH only when changed, stamp
  `df_status_synced_at`. Safe to re-run.
- **Hosting — recommended:** a small **scheduled Cloud Run job in the DesignFlow GCP project** — it
  already has VPC access to read DF data and can call Directus over the public internet. Keeps the bridge
  next to the source of truth.
- **Alternative:** a scheduled **Directus Flow** that calls a DesignFlow read endpoint and updates
  products (keeps the "poppim pulls" model, no new GCP infra; heavier for ~9k rows).

## 7. Interfaces

- **DesignFlow read:** reuse the existing licensing data path — the `licensing_tracking` grid already
  gets latest status + history from the tracking backend (`/lic/getLic`, port 5002). Phase-0 can add a
  lean bulk endpoint, e.g. `GET /lic/status-export` returning `{item_num_id, compan_code, div_code,
  latest_status, brand_assurance, sample_no, updated_at}` — cheaper than reading the full grid payload.
- **poppim write:** Directus REST/SDK `PATCH /items/product/:id` with a **service-account static token**
  (a dedicated low-privilege Directus user that can only update the `df_*` fields). Batch via
  `PATCH /items/product` with multiple ids.

## 8. Open inputs to confirm before building

1. **poppim stage list** — export the Directus `stage` collection (name, stage_order, business_unit) to
   finalize column 3 of §4. (Phase-0 doesn't need it if we only write the read-only field + badge.)
2. **DesignFlow `LicenseFeedBacks` catalog** — `SELECT id, phase, status, "order" FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order";`
   to finalize column 1 of §4 and confirm every status maps.
3. **Directus service token / write auth** — how the bridge authenticates to write `df_*` (static token
   on a dedicated service user is the cleanest; the field-token UI was not reachable, so this may need a
   Directus admin / the `u2giants/directus` repo).
4. **Hosting decision** — Cloud Run job (recommended) vs Directus Flow.

## 9. Edge cases

- **Truncated/garbled poppim item-nos (~0.7%)** — leave unmatched; log them for a manual cleanup list.
- **367 poppim products sharing an item-no** — all receive the same DF status (correct: they're the same
  underlying product/re-offers).
- **~8,551 DesignFlow items not in poppim** — out of Phase-0 scope (mirror only updates existing poppim
  products). Whether to create poppim products for them is a separate decision.
- **company often NULL in DF** (`compan_code_fk` was NULL in the export) — composite key tolerates NULLs;
  fall back to `(item_num_id, div_code_fk)` then most-recent.

## 10. Rollout & rollback

- Backfill `linked_external_id` first (idempotent, reversible — it's a new field).
- Run the mirror in **dry-run/log-only** for one cycle; spot-check 20 products against the DesignFlow grid.
- Enable writes to the `df_*` fields. Rollback = stop the job + (optionally) null the `df_*` fields; no
  DesignFlow data is ever touched.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Licensing Tracking ↔ poppim PM — system overlap & integration direction

**Status:** analysis complete; shared-key **verification IN PROGRESS** (see `HANDOFF.md`).
**Date:** 2026-06-13/14. A fuller strategy write-up exists as an out-of-repo working artifact
(`licensing-integration-strategy.md` in the workspace root); this file is the durable in-repo summary.

## Why this exists

The `licensing_tracking` module (this repo, `src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/`) and a separate
project-management system **poppim** (`github.com/u2giants/poppim-web`, a React + Directus app at
`pm.designflow.app`) **both track the same licensed products and their approval status** — i.e. the
same work is entered in two places. This documents what each system is, where they overlap, the chosen
direction, and the key-matching finding that gates any integration.

## The two systems

- **DesignFlow `licensing_tracking` = the structured, production licensor-approval tracker.**
  One grid row = a product/SKU (`itemHeader`). The approval history is the `licensingStatus` event log;
  the valid phase/status catalog is `LicenseFeedBacks`; Brand Assurance # is `udf_freeform_03`; licensor/
  artist/for-customer are merch-group UDF FKs (`udf_merchgroup05/09/15`). It models dual **Package vs Art**
  submission tracks and assignee / tagged-group routing. Backed by the GCP Cloud SQL `designflow` schema,
  fed from the **Coldlion ERP** via `designflow-data-syncing`. (Built on the RFQ costing chassis, but the
  default grid shows approval/status columns, not pricing.)

- **poppim = the company-wide ClickUp replacement** (Directus backend, separate Postgres). Today its
  licensing model is essentially a single `stage` *name* per product — **no submission / Brand Assurance /
  PPS / costing data model.** ~16.5K products were imported from ClickUp (POP Creations 15,094 / Spruce 1,440).

## Infra reality (matters for any bridge)

The two run on **entirely separate clouds and databases** (DesignFlow = GCP Cloud Run / Cloud SQL;
poppim = Coolify + DigitalOcean, behind Directus at `data.designflow.app`). There is **no existing
integration and no shared key.** Any bridge crosses a network boundary (**API ↔ API**), not a shared DB —
do not assume a SQL join across the two is possible.

## Chosen direction (Albert, 2026-06-13)

**DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim consumes a read-only copy.**
This is a split-by-concern, not a merge: DesignFlow keeps the structured approval (and costing) truth,
poppim is the PM viewing/working surface. The richer, in-use model already lives in DesignFlow, so it is
the source; poppim's coarse stage column becomes a downstream reflection.

## Shared-key finding (poppim side measured 2026-06-14)

- poppim **`product.code` is a dead end** — all values are lowercase ClickUp slugs (6–9 chars), unrelated
  to DesignFlow.
- The **DesignFlow `item_num_id` is embedded as the prefix of poppim `product.name`**, e.g.
  `"MTP78DYNX02 Disney Block MDF perpetual calendar …"`. The token format matches
  `itemHeader.item_num_id` (≈11-char uppercase alphanumeric, same shape as `MEZH1DYPN02`).
- **Coverage:** 9,660 / 16,534 rows (58%) carry a parseable item number; **9,293 distinct**; 367 item-nos
  appear on >1 product (re-offers/duplicates).
- The ~42% with no code are **mostly not products** — ClickUp comments / routing notes / presentation cards
  (e.g. *"Update Status & Licensor Comments in DesignFlow"*, *"Packaging concept submitted 1/10. OPA#114121811"*)
  plus a few genuine early-stage concepts never SKU'd in the PLM. They were never going to match.
- **Implication for the bridge:** key on the item number **parsed from `product.name`** (not `product.code`),
  filter out the non-product rows, and ideally backfill poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` with the
  DesignFlow item number as a one-time cleanup so future joins don't depend on name-parsing.

To reproduce the poppim side: export the Directus `product` collection to CSV and take the first
whitespace-delimited token of `name` where it matches `/^[A-Z0-9]{9,13}$/` with ≥2 digits and ≥3 letters.

## Open verification (blocks go/no-go)

Need DesignFlow's `item_num_id` list (developer-run read-only queries, in `HANDOFF.md`) to **intersect with
the 9,293 poppim item-nos** and confirm true overlap, plus whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs
`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`. See `HANDOFF.md` → "Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration".

input.new_string:
## Verification result (2026-06-14) — GO

DesignFlow's `item_num_id` list (developer queries A/B/C) was intersected with the 9,293 poppim item-nos:

- **9,224 / 9,293 (99.3%)** poppim item-nos exist in DesignFlow; the ~0.7% misses are truncated/garbled
  codes from the ClickUp import.
- DesignFlow has **17,775 distinct** item-nos; **8,551 (48%)** are *not* in poppim (older/archived/
  never-in-ClickUp items — DesignFlow is the superset).
- Matches are **almost entirely licensed** (div 1/2/8 = 9,214; generic div 9 = 10) — confirming this is a
  licensed-product bridge.
- `item_num_id` is **not unique alone** (query A: 19,321 codes → 17,775 distinct; ~1,500 collisions; plus
  367 poppim products share an item-no). The bridge keys on **`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`**.

Conclusion: the embedded item number is a reliable join key — a read-only status mirror is viable. Design:
`licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`.

- Matches are **almost entirely licensed** (div 1/2/8 = 9,214; generic div 9 = 10) — confirming this is a
  licensed-product bridge.
- `item_num_id` is **not unique alone** (query A: 19,321 codes → 17,775 distinct; ~1,500 collisions; plus
  367 poppim products share an item-no). The bridge keys on **`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`**.

Conclusion: the embedded item number is a reliable join key — a read-only status mirror is viable. Design:
`licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`.

# Licensing Tracking ↔ poppim PM — system overlap & integration direction

**Status:** analysis complete; shared-key **verified GO — 99.3% match** (2026-06-14). Phase-0 bridge
design is in `licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`.
**Date:** 2026-06-13/14. A fuller strategy write-up exists as an out-of-repo working artifact
(`licensing-integration-strategy.md` in the workspace root); this file is the durable in-repo summary.

## Why this exists

The `licensing_tracking` module (this repo, `src/app/pages/licensing_tracking/`) and a separate
project-management system **poppim** (`github.com/u2giants/poppim-web`, a React + Directus app at
`pm.designflow.app`) **both track the same licensed products and their approval status** — i.e. the
same work is entered in two places. This documents what each system is, where they overlap, the chosen
direction, and the key-matching finding that gates any integration.

## The two systems

- **DesignFlow `licensing_tracking` = the structured, production licensor-approval tracker.**
  One grid row = a product/SKU (`itemHeader`). The approval history is the `licensingStatus` event log;
  the valid phase/status catalog is `LicenseFeedBacks`; Brand Assurance # is `udf_freeform_03`; licensor/
  artist/for-customer are merch-group UDF FKs (`udf_merchgroup05/09/15`). It models dual **Package vs Art**
  submission tracks and assignee / tagged-group routing. Backed by the GCP Cloud SQL `designflow` schema,
  fed from the **Coldlion ERP** via `designflow-data-syncing`. (Built on the RFQ costing chassis, but the
  default grid shows approval/status columns, not pricing.)

- **poppim = the company-wide ClickUp replacement** (Directus backend, separate Postgres). Today its
  licensing model is essentially a single `stage` *name* per product — **no submission / Brand Assurance /
  PPS / costing data model.** ~16.5K products were imported from ClickUp (POP Creations 15,094 / Spruce 1,440).

## Infra reality (matters for any bridge)

The two run on **entirely separate clouds and databases** (DesignFlow = GCP Cloud Run / Cloud SQL;
poppim = Coolify + DigitalOcean, behind Directus at `data.designflow.app`). There is **no existing
integration and no shared key.** Any bridge crosses a network boundary (**API ↔ API**), not a shared DB —
do not assume a SQL join across the two is possible.

## Chosen direction (Albert, 2026-06-13)

**DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim consumes a read-only copy.**
This is a split-by-concern, not a merge: DesignFlow keeps the structured approval (and costing) truth,
poppim is the PM viewing/working surface. The richer, in-use model already lives in DesignFlow, so it is
the source; poppim's coarse stage column becomes a downstream reflection.

## Shared-key finding (poppim side measured 2026-06-14)

- poppim **`product.code` is a dead end** — all values are lowercase ClickUp slugs (6–9 chars), unrelated
  to DesignFlow.
- The **DesignFlow `item_num_id` is embedded as the prefix of poppim `product.name`**, e.g.
  `"MTP78DYNX02 Disney Block MDF perpetual calendar …"`. The token format matches
  `itemHeader.item_num_id` (≈11-char uppercase alphanumeric, same shape as `MEZH1DYPN02`).
- **Coverage:** 9,660 / 16,534 rows (58%) carry a parseable item number; **9,293 distinct**; 367 item-nos
  appear on >1 product (re-offers/duplicates).
- The ~42% with no code are **mostly not products** — ClickUp comments / routing notes / presentation cards
  (e.g. *"Update Status & Licensor Comments in DesignFlow"*, *"Packaging concept submitted 1/10. OPA#114121811"*)
  plus a few genuine early-stage concepts never SKU'd in the PLM. They were never going to match.
- **Implication for the bridge:** key on the item number **parsed from `product.name`** (not `product.code`),
  filter out the non-product rows, and ideally backfill poppim's `product_link.linked_external_id` with the
  DesignFlow item number as a one-time cleanup so future joins don't depend on name-parsing.

To reproduce the poppim side: export the Directus `product` collection to CSV and take the first
whitespace-delimited token of `name` where it matches `/^[A-Z0-9]{9,13}$/` with ≥2 digits and ≥3 letters.

## Open verification (blocks go/no-go)

Need DesignFlow's `item_num_id` list (developer-run read-only queries, in `HANDOFF.md`) to **intersect with
the 9,293 poppim item-nos** and confirm true overlap, plus whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs
`(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`. See `HANDOFF.md` → "Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration".

## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — shared-key verification (updated 2026-06-14)

**Status:** BLOCKED — waiting on a read-only DesignFlow DB export from the PLM developer.

**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md`. Albert's direction (2026-06-13):
DesignFlow stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (the Directus PM app at
`pm.designflow.app`) consumes a **read-only** copy. Before any bridge is built, confirm the same products
carry a matchable key in both systems.

**Done (this session):**
- Deep-dived both data models (summary in the docs file); confirmed the two run on **separate clouds/DBs
  with no existing integration and no shared key** — any bridge is API↔API, not a SQL join.
- Measured the poppim side from a Directus `product` CSV export: the join key is the DesignFlow
  `item_num_id` embedded as the **prefix of poppim `product.name`** (NOT `product.code`, which is a ClickUp
  slug). **9,293 distinct** item-nos across **58%** of 16,534 rows; 367 duplicates; the ~42% without a code
  are mostly non-product ClickUp noise.

**Next action:**
1. Have the developer run queries **A/B/C** below against the DesignFlow Postgres (read-only) and return
   query **C** as CSV.
2. Intersect DesignFlow `item_num_id` (C) with the 9,293 poppim item-nos → **true overlap %** (go/no-go).
3. Query **A** tells whether `item_num_id` is unique alone or needs `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
4. Produce the match report; if overlap is high, design the read-only status bridge keyed on the item number.

**Queries for the developer** (replace `designflow` if the schema-discovery query returns another schema):

```sql
-- schema check
SELECT table_schema FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_name = 'itemHeader';

-- A) totals, blank codes, and whether the code is unique on its own
SELECT count(*) AS total_rows, count(item_num_id) AS non_null_codes,
       count(DISTINCT item_num_id) AS distinct_codes,
       count(DISTINCT (item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)) AS distinct_code_company_div
FROM designflow."itemHeader";

-- B) breakdown by division (1/2/8 = licensed, 9 = generic)
SELECT div_code_fk, count(*) AS rows FROM designflow."itemHeader" GROUP BY div_code_fk ORDER BY rows DESC;

-- C) the code list to match against poppim (export as CSV)
SELECT item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk, item_displ_descr_name
FROM designflow."itemHeader"
WHERE item_num_id IS NOT NULL AND item_num_id <> ''
ORDER BY item_id_pk DESC;
```

**Risks / watchouts:**
- `item_num_id` is `STRING(100)` and company/division-scoped — the same code may repeat across
  company/division, so the bridge key may need `(item_num_id + company + division)`, not `item_num_id` alone
  (confirm with query A).
- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.

## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — Phase-0 bridge (updated 2026-06-14)

**Status:** shared-key verification **DONE (GO, 99.3%)**; Phase-0 bridge **designed, not yet built**.

**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md` (analysis + verified result) and
`docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md` (the build spec). Albert's direction (2026-06-13): DesignFlow
stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (Directus app at `pm.designflow.app`) consumes a
**read-only** copy.

**Done (this session):**
- Confirmed both data models; the two run on separate clouds/DBs (API↔API, no shared DB).
- **Verified the join key (GO):** DesignFlow `item_num_id` ⇄ the item-number prefix of poppim
  `product.name` (NOT `product.code`). **9,224 / 9,293 (99.3%)** poppim item-nos match DesignFlow; misses
  are truncated codes. Matches are almost entirely licensed (div 1/2/8). Key is **not** unique on
  `item_num_id` alone → use `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
- Wrote the Phase-0 bridge spec (read-only, one-way DesignFlow→poppim status mirror).

**Next action (build the bridge — see `docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`):**
1. Collect the 4 open inputs (spec §8): export the poppim `stage` collection; run
   `SELECT id, phase, status, "order" FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order";`; obtain a
   Directus service-account write token; pick hosting (Cloud Run job vs Directus Flow).
2. Add the read-only `df_*` fields + backfill `product_link.linked_external_id` in the `u2giants/directus`
   schema repo.
3. Build the bulk read endpoint on DesignFlow (`/lic/status-export`) and the idempotent sync job.
4. Dry-run/log-only one cycle, spot-check 20 products, then enable writes.

**Risks / watchouts:**
- Key needs the `(item_num_id + company + division)` composite (`item_num_id` alone has ~1,500 DF
  collisions; 367 poppim products share an item-no). `compan_code_fk` is often NULL — tolerate it.
- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.

content.text:
Both done. Here's where things stand.

## ✅ Result recorded + PR refreshed
[designflow-frontend#124](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/124) now reflects the verified outcome (commit `166cbacd`):
- The integration doc and HANDOFF.md flipped from *"blocked / pending"* → **verified GO (99.3%)**, with the full match numbers, the composite-key requirement, and the licensed-only finding.
- AGENTS.md pending-work + doc-map rows updated to match.

## ✅ Phase-0 bridge designed
New spec: [docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md](dflow/designflow-frontend/docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md). The short version of the design:

- **Scope:** one-way, read-only mirror of each licensed product's approval status from DesignFlow → poppim. poppim never writes back. Licensed products only.
- **Least-disruptive default:** write the DesignFlow status into a **read-only field + badge** on the poppim product — *don't* overwrite the user-draggable board stage yet (that competes with manual drags; it's a Phase-2 flag). This still stops the double-entry: the real status shows up automatically, so nobody re-keys it.
- **Key:** join on `item_num_id` (parsed from `product.name`) + company + division; backfill it into poppim's existing `linked_external_id` so future syncs don't parse names.
- **Mechanism:** a small scheduled, idempotent job (recommended: a Cloud Run job in the DesignFlow GCP project; alternative: a Directus Flow). Reads a lean DesignFlow status-export endpoint, PATCHes poppim via a service-account token.
- **Spelled out in the spec:** the status→field/stage mapping (provisional), the `df_*` schema additions, edge cases (the 0.7% garbled codes, 367 shared item-nos, 8,551 DF-only items, NULL company), and a dry-run→verify→enable rollout with a clean rollback (DesignFlow is never touched).

## What's needed before anyone *builds* it
The spec ends with four concrete inputs (it lists them as §8) — none are blockers for the design, just the things a builder needs:
1. Export the poppim **`stage`** collection (to finalize the status→stage mapping).
2. One more dev query for the DesignFlow **`LicenseFeedBacks`** catalog (the full status list).
3. A Directus **service-account write token** (the per-user token UI you couldn't find — this likely needs a Directus admin or the `u2giants/directus` repo).
4. A **hosting decision** (Cloud Run job vs Directus Flow).

Nothing else pending on my end. When you want to move from design into building, grab those four and I'll take it from there — or I can draft the Directus schema changes and the sync-job skeleton now if you'd like a running start.

content: i want to fully implement this end-to-end. what's the best way to do that?

message.content: i want to fully implement this end-to-end. what's the best way to do that?

content.text:
Great — let's do it properly. Before writing code, let me be straight about what "end-to-end" actually spans here, because the *best* way is shaped by it: this bridge crosses **two clouds, two-plus repos (one of which — `u2giants/directus` — isn't even checked out here), needs credentials I don't have, and modifies poppim — a shared production system the whole company uses.** So the best approach isn't "I code it all in one go"; it's **build the self-contained core now and test it end-to-end against non-prod, while we unblock the few access-gated and sign-off pieces, then roll out in safe stages.**

## One architecture refinement first (it simplifies things a lot)
My spec proposed "new DesignFlow endpoint + external job." There's a cleaner option: **a single Cloud Run Job inside the DesignFlow GCP project that reads Cloud SQL directly** (via the existing `designflow-tracking` Sequelize models — same DB access the app already uses) and writes to poppim via the Directus API. That removes a whole component (no new HTTP endpoint, no second auth hop). It lives in the `designflow-tracking` repo, reuses its DB secrets and Cloud Build, and the **only new credential is a Directus write token.** I'd build it on `sandbox-albert` like everything else.

## What it actually takes — and who owns each piece
| # | Piece | Who | Gated on |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sync job** — read DF status → map → write poppim (in `designflow-tracking`) | **Me** | a Directus token + DF data to read |
| 2 | **Backfill** `linked_external_id` from `product.name` (one-time script) | **Me** | Directus admin token |
| 3 | **Directus schema** — add `df_*` fields to `product` | **Me, IF** I get the `u2giants/directus` repo + token | poppim owner |
| 4 | **Finalize status→stage mapping** | Me | the 2 enumerations (poppim `stage` export + `LicenseFeedBacks` query) |
| 5 | **Deploy** Cloud Run Job + Cloud Scheduler (DF GCP project) | **You / the developer** | GCP access |
| 6 | **Production go-live** (enable writes into the live poppim) | **You + poppim owner** | sign-off on changing a shared prod system |

The good news: **I can build and fully test #1–#4 locally** (DF read against your local/sandbox DB + Directus write against a non-prod token) without touching production. Deploy (#5) and go-live (#6) are the genuinely access-gated steps.

## The honest blocker
Item #3/#6: poppim isn't yours alone to change — adding fields and writing into ~9,000 live products on someone else's Directus/Coolify infra needs the poppim owner's OK and the `u2giants/directus` repo. If you own/admin that, we're clear. If not, that coordination is the real long pole, not the code.

To start building the core immediately, I need very little — but a few decisions change *what* I build:

content.text:
That last answer is the most important thing you've told me — it changes the recommendation, so let me fold it in rather than barrel ahead.

**Your deploy reality (push to GitHub → Cloud Build trigger → deploy) doesn't fit a Cloud Run *Job*.** Those triggers deploy the existing Cloud Run *services*; a scheduled Job + Cloud Scheduler is a separate resource someone has to create by hand in GCP once — and you just told me nobody on the team is set up to do that. Forcing the job onto GCP would strand it.

So here's the refinement: **put the scheduled worker on the side you actually control (poppim/Directus on Coolify), and give DesignFlow only a tiny read-only endpoint that ships through your normal push-to-deploy.** Concretely:

- **DesignFlow side (you push, Cloud Build auto-deploys — no manual GCP):** I add one authenticated `GET /lic/status-export` route to the existing `designflow-tracking` service. Pushing it to `sandbox-albert` auto-deploys to your tracking sandbox, exactly like every other change. No new GCP resource, no gcloud.
- **poppim side (you control Coolify + Directus):** the actual sync worker lives here — a small scheduled process that calls that DF endpoint, maps the status, and batch-updates products. You can deploy and schedule it yourself, the same way poppim already ships.

Net: each half is deployed by whoever already knows how to deploy it. That's the genuinely best fit for your setup.

Let me start on the unblocked work right now — cloning your Directus repo so I can ground the schema changes:

content.text:
Now I have the full picture, and it's good news: **your `directus` repo already runs exactly this kind of thing.** Scheduled Node workers live in `pm-system/sync/` (e.g. `entra-role-sync.mjs`) and deploy as **systemd service+timer pairs** (`popcrm-contact-sync.service`/`.timer`). Schema changes are `pm-system/add-*-model.mjs` scripts; backfills are `pm-system/migration/backfill-*.mjs`. My whole poppim side drops into these existing slots — no new infrastructure, nothing exotic. Auth is via `DX_URL` + `DX_ADMIN_EMAIL`/`DX_ADMIN_PASSWORD` (an env file), same as the other workers.

## The concrete end-to-end build

**Side A — DesignFlow (`designflow-tracking`; you push, Cloud Build auto-deploys to sandbox):**
- `GET /lic/status-export` — authed read endpoint returning licensed items: `{item_num_id, company, division, latest_status, brand_assurance, sample_no, updated_at}`. Reuses the latest-status query the licensing grid already runs. + unit tests. *(I build this on `sandbox-albert`, PR to develop — your normal flow.)*

**Side B — poppim (`directus` repo; you deploy, modeled 1:1 on existing scripts):**
- `pm-system/add-designflow-mirror-fields.mjs` → adds read-only `df_license_status`, `df_brand_assurance`, `df_sample_no`, `df_status_synced_at` to `product` (like `add-collaboration-model.mjs`).
- `pm-system/migration/backfill-designflow-itemno.mjs` → parses the item-no out of `product.name` into `linked_external_id` (like `backfill-clickup-status-fields.mjs`).
- `pm-system/sync/designflow-status-sync.mjs` → pulls Side A, maps status, batch-updates matched products, idempotent, dry-run flag (like `entra-role-sync.mjs`).
- `pm-system/systemd/poppim-designflow-sync.service` + `.timer` → runs it every ~15 min (like `popcrm-contact-sync.*`).

**Rollout:** apply fields → backfill dry-run → sync log-only → spot-check 20 → enable writes → schedule → promote to prod.

## What I need to start building for real (small)
1. **Local Postgres password** (your local DesignFlow DB) — with it I'll pull the `LicenseFeedBacks` status catalog *and* test the `/lic/status-export` endpoint against real data myself, no developer round-trip. (Or point me at a DesignFlow sandbox DB.)
2. **poppim/Directus non-prod access** — `DX_URL` + `DX_ADMIN_EMAIL` + `DX_ADMIN_PASSWORD` for a **non-prod** Directus. With it I apply the fields, run the backfill, and build the worker. I'll pull the `stage` list myself.

**One question that shapes #2:** is there a separate **non-prod** Directus instance, or just the one at `data.designflow.app`? If it's single-instance, no problem — I'll work against it through a dedicated low-privilege "sync" service user with everything in dry-run until you approve writes, and the new `df_*` fields are additive and reversible (they can't disturb existing data).

Hand me those two credentials (and the non-prod answer) and I'll start building immediately — beginning with Side A, which I can write and unit-test right away. Want me to go ahead and scaffold Side A now while you gather them?

content.text:
Honest answer: **yes for the easy part, and I know exactly how to do the hard part — but I don't have the two real lists yet to write the final version, and part of it is genuinely your call, not something I can read from code.** Let me separate the two things people lump together as "mapping," because they're very different in difficulty:

## 1. Field mapping — mechanical, I know this now
This is just "which DesignFlow value lands in which poppim field." One-to-one, no judgment:

| DesignFlow (licensing tracking) | → poppim `product` field |
|---|---|
| item number (`item_num_id`) | `linked_external_id` (the join key) |
| latest licensing status | `df_license_status` (shown as a badge) |
| Brand Assurance # | `df_brand_assurance` |
| Sample # | `df_sample_no` |

Done. No ambiguity.

## 2. Status → board-stage mapping — this is the hard, judgment part
DesignFlow's licensing tracking isn't a single status column — it's an **event log** (every submission/approval event) drawn from a **catalog of ~15–20 fine-grained statuses**, and it runs on **two parallel tracks** (artwork/concept *and* packaging). poppim's board, by contrast, has a **handful of coarse stages**. So mapping DesignFlow → a poppim stage is **lossy** — many DesignFlow statuses collapse into one poppim column. Roughly what I *believe* the DesignFlow statuses are (read from the code, **not yet confirmed against your live list**):

> Ready to Submit · Concept Submitted · Packaging Concept Submitted · Concept Approved · Concept Not Approved/Revision · Request Pre-Pro Sample · Pre-Pro Submitted · Pre-Pro Approved · (Production Approved/Closed)

The trouble: deciding that, say, *"Concept Approved"* should move a card to your **"Approved"** column vs. leave it in **"Licensor Review"** is a **business decision about how you want the board to read** — only you/Liz/Jessica can rule on that. I can propose it; I can't *know* it.

## The honest gap, and why it doesn't block us
I've been working off **inferred** statuses (from code) and **mock** poppim stages — I have **not** seen your two real lists:
1. DesignFlow's actual `LicenseFeedBacks` status catalog, and
2. poppim's actual `stage` collection.

**And here's the good news: Phase-0 doesn't need the hard mapping at all.** Instead of overwriting the board stage (lossy, and it'd fight people dragging cards), we **show DesignFlow's real status verbatim** in the `df_license_status` badge. The board stays as-is; the true status just appears next to it, auto-updated. No lossy mapping, no risk to the existing board. The status→stage mapping becomes a *later, optional* step you and the team review when you decide you want the board itself to move automatically.

## So, to make the mapping real
Since you're sole admin, two things unblock me to pull the actual lists and produce a concrete, reviewable mapping:
1. **Your local Postgres password** → I dump the real DesignFlow `LicenseFeedBacks` catalog (exact statuses + order).
2. **Your Directus admin login** (`DX_URL` + email + password) → I pull the real poppim `stage` collection.

With those, I'll put the **actual** DesignFlow statuses next to your **actual** poppim stages and show you a proposed mapping table to approve — and for Phase-0 we may not even use it. Want to hand me those so I can replace my guesses with your real data?

content.text:
Good — you being sole Directus/Coolify admin clears the ownership question entirely. Two quick clarifications:

## 1. "Which app's local Postgres?" → **dflow (DesignFlow)**
The local PostgreSQL on your machine (`localhost:5432`, user `postgres`) is the one holding the **DesignFlow** schema — the `designflow_dev` / `designflow_sandbox` you seeded locally. That's where `LicenseFeedBacks` (the status catalog I need) lives. **poppim's database isn't local** — it's the Directus Postgres on Coolify, which I reach through Directus, not psql.

So: I need the password to your **local DesignFlow Postgres**. If you're not sure it has the data or don't know the password, the fallback is one more tiny read-only query for the developer:
```sql
SELECT id, phase, status, "order", item_order FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order", item_order;
```

## 2. The Directus account — small chicken-and-egg
I **can't make one for myself.** Creating a Directus user requires already being logged in as an admin, and your only admin login is **Microsoft SSO in your browser** — which I can't drive headlessly. So it has to go the other way: **you create a service account in the Directus UI (you're already logged in via SSO), then give me its email + password.** It's quick:

> Directus → **User Directory** (people icon) → **+ Create User** → set First name `designflow-sync`, an **Email** like `designflow-sync@designflow.app`, a **Password** you make up → set **Role = Administrator** (you can tighten later) → **Save**. Then paste me that email + password.

I'll use it only for this integration, and you can delete the user when we're done — treat it as disposable. (The existing `pm-system` workers already log in this exact way, with `DX_ADMIN_EMAIL` + `DX_ADMIN_PASSWORD`, so this fits how your system already runs.)

## But for the mapping *right now*, you don't even need #2
To just produce the mapping table, I only need the two **lists** — and you can hand me the poppim one with **zero credentials**, the same way you exported products:

> Directus → **Content** → open the **`stage`** collection → **Export** (the export icon) → Format **CSV**, fields `name, stage_order, category, business_unit` → it lands in **File Library** → download → drop it here.

So the fastest path to answer your original question:
- **You:** export the `stage` CSV (above), and either give me the **local DF Postgres password** or have the developer run that one `LicenseFeedBacks` query.
- **Me:** put your real DesignFlow statuses next to your real poppim stages and show you a concrete proposed mapping to approve.

The service-account login (#2) we only need once we move from *designing* the mapping to *building* the sync. Want to start with the two lists?

content.text:
Good instinct — yes, the sync will need **ongoing** read access to DesignFlow forever, so this is the right thing to nail down. But the best answer is **not a database password.**

## The right way: an authenticated read-only endpoint, not DB credentials

The durable, secure way to give the sync permanent read access is the **`/lic/status-export` endpoint** that's already Side A of the plan — a small read-only URL on your DesignFlow tracking service that returns *only* the licensing fields, guarded by a secret key. It's better than a DB password on every axis:

- **You deploy it yourself** via your normal "push to GitHub → Cloud Build" flow — no GCP database gymnastics.
- **It exposes only what the sync needs** (status, Brand Assurance, sample #, item #) — not your entire database.
- **It's permanent and revocable** — rotate the secret key any time; no standing DB login floating around.
- It's the *same* thing the sync worker calls every 15 minutes anyway, so we build it once and it serves both the mapping and the ongoing sync.

A raw read-only **DB password** is possible, but it's the worse option: your DesignFlow Postgres is private inside GCP, so handing out access means your developer has to set up Cloud SQL networking + a read-only role + a proxy — more work, and it exposes the *whole* database, not just licensing. I'd steer away from it.

## One honesty note
Building that endpoint will need your developer **once** — to store the secret key in GCP (you don't have that access, they do). After that it's yours, permanent, and deployed by your pushes. So it's "developer once to set up, then never again," versus "developer sets up DB access *and* you're sharing a live DB login ongoing."

## For the mapping *right now*, you don't need to set up any access at all
Designing the mapping just needs two static lists. Get them the easy way — no password, no endpoint yet:

1. **DesignFlow statuses** — have the developer run this one read-only query and send the result (it's tiny):
   ```sql
   SELECT id, phase, status, "order", item_order FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order", item_order;
   ```
2. **poppim stages** — you export it yourself, zero credentials, like the product CSV:
   > Directus → **Content** → **`stage`** collection → **Export** → CSV (`name, stage_order, category, business_unit`) → File Library → download → drop it here.

Send me those two and I'll produce the real, side-by-side mapping for you to approve. Then, when we move from designing to building, we set up the permanent endpoint (the secure "ongoing read-only access" you're asking about) as part of Side A.

Want to grab those two lists?
